title: Indigenous peoples of the Americas

infobox.key: [Saint Vincent and the Grenadines](/wiki/Saint_Vincent_and_the_Grenadines "Saint Vincent and the Grenadines")
[Saint Kitts and Nevis](/wiki/Saint_Kitts_and_Nevis "Saint Kitts and Nevis")

infobox.value: [English](/wiki/English_language "English language") **·** [Portuguese](/wiki/Portuguese_language "Portuguese language") **·** [French](/wiki/French_language "French language") **·** [Danish](/wiki/Danish_language "Danish language") **·** [Dutch](/wiki/Dutch_language "Dutch language") **·** [Russian](/wiki/Russian_language "Russian language") (formerly spoken in [Russian Alaska](/wiki/Russian_colonization_of_North_America "Russian colonization of North America")) | |

are the peoples who are native to [the Americas](/wiki/The_Americas "The Americas") or the [Western Hemisphere](/wiki/Western_Hemisphere "Western Hemisphere"). Their ancestors are among the [pre-Columbian population](/wiki/Pre-Columbian_population "Pre-Columbian population") of [South](/wiki/South_America "South America") or [North America](/wiki/North_America "North America"), including [Central America](/wiki/Central_America "Central America") and the [Caribbean](/wiki/Caribbean "Caribbean"). Indigenous peoples live throughout the Americas. While often minorities in their countries, Indigenous peoples are the majority in [Greenland](/wiki/Indigenous_peoples_in_Greenland "Indigenous peoples in Greenland")[[37]](#cite_note-cia-38) and close to a majority in [Bolivia](/wiki/Indigenous_peoples_in_Bolivia "Indigenous peoples in Bolivia")[[38]](#cite_note-39) and [Guatemala](/wiki/Indigenous_peoples_in_Guatemala "Indigenous peoples in Guatemala").[[39]](#cite_note-40)

| Languages | |
| Numerous [Indigenous American languages](/wiki/Indigenous_languages_of_the_Americas "Indigenous languages of the Americas") (both extant and extinct) Colonial European languages: [Spanish](/wiki/Spanish_language "Spanish language") **·** [English](/wiki/English_language "English language") **·** [Portuguese](/wiki/Portuguese_language "Portuguese language") **·** [French](/wiki/French_language "French language") **·** [Danish](/wiki/Danish_language "Danish language") **·** [Dutch](/wiki/Dutch_language "Dutch language") **·** [Russian](/wiki/Russian_language "Russian language") (formerly spoken in [Russian Alaska](/wiki/Russian_colonization_of_North_America "Russian colonization of North America")) | |

| Mostly [Christianity](/wiki/Christianity "Christianity") ([Catholicism](/wiki/Catholic_Church "Catholic Church") **·** [Protestantism](/wiki/Protestantism "Protestantism") **·** [Eastern Orthodoxy](/wiki/Eastern_Orthodoxy "Eastern Orthodoxy") **·** [Native American Church](/wiki/Native_American_Church "Native American Church") **·** [Neo-American Church](/wiki/Neo-American_Church "Neo-American Church") **·** [Restorationism](/wiki/Restorationism "Restorationism")) Minorities still practice various [Indigenous American religions](/wiki/Native_American_religions "Native American religions") and [mythologies](/wiki/Mythologies_of_the_Indigenous_peoples_of_the_Americas "Mythologies of the Indigenous peoples of the Americas")[[35]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHultkrantz1987-36)[[36]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEZerries1987-37) | |

| [Métis](/wiki/M%C3%A9tis "Métis") **·** [Mestiços](/wiki/Mesti%C3%A7o "Mestiço") **·** [Mestizos](/wiki/Mestizo "Mestizo") **·** [Zambos](/wiki/Zambo "Zambo") **·** [Pardos](/wiki/Pardo "Pardo") | |

The **Indigenous peoples of the Americas** are the peoples who are native to [the Americas](/wiki/The_Americas "The Americas") or the [Western Hemisphere](/wiki/Western_Hemisphere "Western Hemisphere"). Their ancestors are among the [pre-Columbian population](/wiki/Pre-Columbian_population "Pre-Columbian population") of [South](/wiki/South_America "South America") or [North America](/wiki/North_America "North America"), including [Central America](/wiki/Central_America "Central America") and the [Caribbean](/wiki/Caribbean "Caribbean"). Indigenous peoples live throughout the Americas. While often minorities in their countries, Indigenous peoples are the majority in [Greenland](/wiki/Indigenous_peoples_in_Greenland "Indigenous peoples in Greenland")[[37]](#cite_note-cia-38) and close to a majority in [Bolivia](/wiki/Indigenous_peoples_in_Bolivia "Indigenous peoples in Bolivia")[[38]](#cite_note-39) and [Guatemala](/wiki/Indigenous_peoples_in_Guatemala "Indigenous peoples in Guatemala").[[39]](#cite_note-40)

There are at least 1,000 different [Indigenous languages of the Americas](/wiki/Indigenous_languages_of_the_Americas "Indigenous languages of the Americas"). Some languages, including [Quechua](/wiki/Quechuan_languages "Quechuan languages"), [Arawak](/wiki/Arawak_language "Arawak language"), [Aymara](/wiki/Aymara_language "Aymara language"), [Guaraní](/wiki/Guarani_language "Guarani language"), [Nahuatl](/wiki/Nahuatl "Nahuatl"), and [some Mayan languages](/wiki/Mayan_languages "Mayan languages"), have millions of speakers and are recognized as official by governments in [Bolivia](/wiki/Bolivia "Bolivia"), [Peru](/wiki/Peru "Peru"), [Paraguay](/wiki/Paraguay "Paraguay"), and [Greenland](/wiki/Greenland "Greenland").

Indigenous peoples, whether residing in rural or urban areas, often maintain aspects of their cultural practices, including [religion](/wiki/Native_American_religions "Native American religions"), [social organization](/wiki/Social_organization "Social organization"), and [subsistence](/wiki/Subsistence_economy "Subsistence economy") practices. Over time, these cultures have evolved, preserving traditional customs while adapting to modern needs. Some Indigenous groups remain relatively isolated from [Western culture](/wiki/Western_culture "Western culture"), with some still classified as [uncontacted peoples](/wiki/Uncontacted_peoples "Uncontacted peoples").

The Americas also host millions of individuals of mixed Indigenous, European, and sometimes African or Asian descent, historically referred to as [mestizos](/wiki/Mestizo "Mestizo") in Spanish-speaking countries.[[40]](#cite_note-41)[[41]](#cite_note-Mangan-42) In many [Latin American](/wiki/Latin_America "Latin America") nations, people of partial Indigenous descent constitute a majority or significant portion of the population, particularly in Central America, Mexico, Peru, Bolivia, [Ecuador](/wiki/Ecuador "Ecuador"), [Colombia](/wiki/Colombia "Colombia"), [Venezuela](/wiki/Venezuela "Venezuela"), [Chile](/wiki/Chile "Chile"), and Paraguay.[[42]](#cite_note-43)[[43]](#cite_note-:8-44)[[44]](#cite_note-45) Mestizos outnumber Indigenous peoples in most Spanish-speaking countries, according to estimates of ethnic cultural identification. However, since Indigenous communities in the Americas are defined by [cultural identification](/wiki/Cultural_identification "Cultural identification") and [kinship](/wiki/Kinship "Kinship") rather than ancestry or [race](/wiki/Race_(human_categorization) "Race (human categorization)"), mestizos are typically not counted among the Indigenous population unless they speak an Indigenous language or identify with a specific Indigenous culture.[[45]](#cite_note-Bartolomé_1996:2-46) Additionally, many individuals of wholly Indigenous descent who do not follow Indigenous traditions or speak an Indigenous language have been classified or self-identified as mestizo due to assimilation into the dominant [Hispanic culture](/wiki/Hispanic_culture "Hispanic culture"). In recent years, the self-identified Indigenous population in many countries has increased as individuals reclaim their heritage amid rising Indigenous-led movements for [self-determination](/wiki/Self-determination "Self-determination") and [social justice](/wiki/Social_justice "Social justice").[[46]](#cite_note-47)

In past centuries, Indigenous peoples had diverse societal, governmental, and subsistence systems. Some Indigenous peoples were historically [hunter-gatherers](/wiki/Hunter-gatherers "Hunter-gatherers"), while others practiced agriculture and [aquaculture](/wiki/Aquaculture "Aquaculture"). Various Indigenous societies developed complex [social structures](/wiki/Social_structure "Social structure"), including precontact monumental architecture, organized cities, [city-states](/wiki/City-state "City-state"), chiefdoms, states, monarchies, republics, confederacies, and empires.[[47]](#cite_note-48) These societies possessed varying levels of knowledge in fields such as [engineering](/wiki/Pre-Columbian_engineering_in_the_Americas "Pre-Columbian engineering in the Americas"), [architecture](/wiki/Pre-Columbian_architecture "Pre-Columbian architecture"), mathematics, astronomy, [writing](/wiki/History_of_writing "History of writing"), physics, medicine, [agriculture](/wiki/Pre-Columbian_agriculture "Pre-Columbian agriculture"), irrigation, geology, mining, metallurgy, [art](/wiki/Pre-Columbian_art "Pre-Columbian art"), [sculpture](/wiki/Indigenous_American_sculpture "Indigenous American sculpture"), and goldsmithing.

## Terminology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Indigenous_peoples_of_the_Americas&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Terminology")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/75/Karte_Karibik_Inseln.png/250px-Karte_Karibik_Inseln.png)](/wiki/File:Karte_Karibik_Inseln.png)

The [West Indies](/wiki/West_Indies "West Indies") (or [Antilles](/wiki/Antilles "Antilles")) in relation to the continental Americas

Further information: [Native American name controversy](/wiki/Native_American_name_controversy "Native American name controversy")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/16/Navajo_%28young_boy%29_2007.jpg/250px-Navajo_%28young_boy%29_2007.jpg)](/wiki/File:Navajo_(young_boy)_2007.jpg)

A [Navajo](/wiki/Navajo "Navajo") boy in the desert in present-day [Monument Valley](/wiki/Monument_Valley "Monument Valley") in [Arizona](/wiki/Arizona "Arizona") with the "Three Sisters" rock formation in the background in 2007

[![American Indian, Alaska Native, and Inuit populations of the Americas in 2024](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/08/Amerindians_and_Inuits_map.png/250px-Amerindians_and_Inuits_map.png)](/wiki/File:Amerindians_and_Inuits_map.png)

Indigenous populations of the Americas in 2024

Application of the term "[Indian](/wiki/Native_American_name_controversy#"Indian"_and_"American_Indian"_(since_1492) "Native American name controversy")" originated with [Christopher Columbus](/wiki/Christopher_Columbus "Christopher Columbus"), who, when searching for India, made landfall in the Americas but thought he had arrived in the [East Indies](/wiki/East_Indies "East Indies").[[48]](#cite_note-49)[[49]](#cite_note-50)[[50]](#cite_note-51)[[51]](#cite_note-52)[[52]](#cite_note-53)[[53]](#cite_note-54)

The islands came to be known as the "[West Indies](/wiki/West_Indies "West Indies")" (or "[Antilles](/wiki/Antilles "Antilles")"), a name that is still used to describe the islands. This led to the blanket term "Indies" and "Indians" ([Spanish](/wiki/Spanish_language "Spanish language"): *indios*; [Portuguese](/wiki/Portuguese_language "Portuguese language"): *índios*; [French](/wiki/French_language "French language"): *indiens*; [Dutch](/wiki/Dutch_language "Dutch language"): *indianen*) for the Indigenous inhabitants, which implied some kind of ethnic or cultural unity among the Indigenous peoples of the Americas. This unifying concept, codified in law, religion, and politics, was not originally accepted by the myriad groups of Indigenous peoples themselves but has since been embraced or tolerated by many over the last two centuries.[[54]](#cite_note-55) The term *First Nations* is used in Canada to identify that type of Indigenous people.

The term "Indian" (or First Nations in Canada) generally does not include the culturally and [linguistically](/wiki/Eskimo-Aleut "Eskimo-Aleut") distinct Indigenous peoples of the Arctic regions of the Americas, including the [Aleuts](/wiki/Aleut "Aleut"), [Inuit](/wiki/Inuit "Inuit"), or [Yupik peoples](/wiki/Yupik_peoples "Yupik peoples"). These peoples entered the continent as a second, more recent wave of migration several thousand years later and have much more recent genetic and cultural commonalities with the [Indigenous peoples of Siberia](/wiki/Indigenous_peoples_of_Siberia "Indigenous peoples of Siberia"). However, these groups are nonetheless considered among the "Indigenous peoples of the Americas".[[55]](#cite_note-Sutton_2021_p._30-56)

The term *Amerindian*, a [portmanteau](/wiki/Portmanteau "Portmanteau") of "American Indian", was coined in 1902 by the [American Anthropological Association](/wiki/American_Anthropological_Association "American Anthropological Association"). It has been controversial ever since its creation. It was immediately rejected by some leading members of the Association, and, while adopted by many, it was never universally accepted.[[56]](#cite_note-AmerIndNYT-57) While never popular in Indigenous communities themselves, it remains a preferred term among some anthropologists, notably in some parts of Canada and the [English-speaking Caribbean](/wiki/Commonwealth_Caribbean "Commonwealth Caribbean").[[57]](#cite_note-58)[[58]](#cite_note-59)[[59]](#cite_note-60)[[60]](#cite_note-:9-61)

"[Indigenous peoples in Canada](/wiki/Indigenous_peoples_in_Canada "Indigenous peoples in Canada")" is used as the collective name for [First Nations](/wiki/First_Nations_in_Canada "First Nations in Canada"), [Inuit](/wiki/Inuit "Inuit"), and [Métis](/wiki/M%C3%A9tis_(Canada) "Métis (Canada)").[[61]](#cite_note-UManitoba-62)[[62]](#cite_note-CBC-Joseph-63) The term *Aboriginal peoples* as a [collective noun](/wiki/Collective_noun "Collective noun") (also describing First Nations, Inuit, and Métis) is a specific [term of art](/wiki/Term_of_art "Term of art") used in some legal documents, including the [Constitution Act, 1982](/wiki/Constitution_Act,_1982 "Constitution Act, 1982").[[63]](#cite_note-Canada-64) Over time, as societal perceptions and government–indigenous relationships have shifted, many historical terms have changed definitions or been replaced as they have fallen out of favor.[[64]](#cite_note-:5-65) The use of the term "Indian" is frowned upon because it represents the imposition and restriction of Indigenous peoples and cultures by the Canadian Government.[[64]](#cite_note-:5-65) The terms "Native" and "[Eskimo](/wiki/Eskimo "Eskimo")" are generally regarded as disrespectful (in Canada), and so are rarely used unless specifically required.[[65]](#cite_note-:6-66) While "Indigenous peoples" is the preferred term, many individuals or communities may choose to describe their identity using a different term.[[64]](#cite_note-:5-65)[[65]](#cite_note-:6-66)

The Métis people of Canada can be contrasted, for instance, to the Indigenous-European mixed-race [mestizos](/wiki/Mestizos "Mestizos") (or *[caboclos](/wiki/Caboclos "Caboclos")* in Brazil) of [Hispanic America](/wiki/Hispanic_America "Hispanic America") whose large populations constitute outright majorities, pluralities, or at the least large minorities in most Latin American countries. They identify largely as an ethnic group distinct from Europeans and Indigenous, but consider themselves a subset of the European-derived [Hispanic](/wiki/Hispanidad "Hispanidad") or [Brazilian peoplehood](/wiki/Brazilians "Brazilians") in culture and ethnicity ([cf.](/wiki/Cf. "Cf.") *[ladinos](/wiki/Ladino_people "Ladino people")*).

Among [Spanish-speaking countries](/wiki/Spanish-speaking_countries "Spanish-speaking countries"), *indígenas* or *pueblos indígenas* ("Indigenous peoples") is a common term, though *nativos* or *pueblos nativos* ('native peoples') may also be heard; moreover, *aborigen* ('aborigine') is used in [Argentina](/wiki/Argentina "Argentina") and *pueblos originarios* ('original peoples') is common in [Chile](/wiki/Chile "Chile"). In Brazil, *indígenas* and *povos originários* ("Indigenous peoples") are common formal-sounding designations, while *índio* ('Indian') is still the more often heard term (the noun for the South-Asian nationality being *indiano*), but since the early 2010s has been considered offensive and pejorative.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] *Aborígene* and *nativo* are rarely used in Brazil in Indigenous-specific contexts (e.g., *aborígene* is usually understood as the ethnonym for [Indigenous Australians](/wiki/Indigenous_Australians "Indigenous Australians")). The Spanish and Portuguese equivalents to *Indian*, nevertheless, could be used to mean any [hunter-gatherer](/wiki/Hunter-gatherer "Hunter-gatherer") or full-blooded Indigenous person, particularly to continents other than Europe or Africa—for example, *[indios filipinos](/wiki/Filipinos#Hispanized_caste_system "Filipinos")*.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Indigenous peoples of the United States are commonly known as [Native Americans](/wiki/Native_Americans_in_the_United_States "Native Americans in the United States"), Indians, as well as [Alaska Natives](/wiki/Alaska_Natives "Alaska Natives").[*[clarification needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Please_clarify "Wikipedia:Please clarify")*] The term "Indian" is still used in some communities and remains in use in the official names of many institutions and businesses in [Indian Country](/wiki/Indian_Country "Indian Country").[[66]](#cite_note-Office-67)

### Name controversy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Indigenous_peoples_of_the_Americas&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Name controversy")]

Main article: [Native American name controversy](/wiki/Native_American_name_controversy "Native American name controversy")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5b/Guajiran_artisans.jpg/250px-Guajiran_artisans.jpg)](/wiki/File:Guajiran_artisans.jpg)

[Wayuu](/wiki/Wayuu_people "Wayuu people") women in the [Guajira Peninsula](/wiki/Guajira_Peninsula "Guajira Peninsula"), which encompasses parts of [Colombia](/wiki/Colombia "Colombia") and [Venezuela](/wiki/Venezuela "Venezuela")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/16/Taquile_Inselfest_Frauen.JPG/250px-Taquile_Inselfest_Frauen.JPG)](/wiki/File:Taquile_Inselfest_Frauen.JPG)

[Quechua](/wiki/Quechua_people "Quechua people") women in festive dress on [Taquile Island](/wiki/Taquile_Island "Taquile Island") on [Lake Titicaca](/wiki/Lake_Titicaca "Lake Titicaca"), west of [Peru](/wiki/Peru "Peru")

The various nations, tribes, and bands of Indigenous peoples of the Americas have differing preferences in terminology for themselves.[[67]](#cite_note-Mann1-68)[*[page needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources "Wikipedia:Citing sources")*] While there are regional and generational variations in which umbrella terms are preferred for Indigenous peoples as a whole, in general, most Indigenous peoples prefer to be identified by the name of their specific nation, tribe, or band.[[67]](#cite_note-Mann1-68)[[68]](#cite_note-InTermGuide-69)

Early settlers often adopted terms that some tribes used for each other, not realizing these were derogatory terms used by enemies. When discussing broader subsets of peoples, naming has often been based on shared language, region, or historical relationship.[[69]](#cite_note-70) Many English [exonyms](/wiki/Exonyms "Exonyms") have been used to refer to the Indigenous peoples of the Americas. Some of these names were based on foreign language terms used by earlier explorers and colonists, while others resulted from the colonists' attempts to translate or transliterate [endonyms](/wiki/Endonyms "Endonyms") from the native languages. Other terms arose during periods of conflict between the colonists and Indigenous peoples.[[70]](#cite_note-71)

Since the late 20th century, Indigenous peoples in the Americas have been more vocal about how they want to be addressed, pushing to suppress the use of terms widely considered to be obsolete, inaccurate, or [racist](/wiki/Racism "Racism"). During the latter half of the 20th century and the rise of the [Indian rights movement](/wiki/Indian_rights_movement "Indian rights movement"), the [United States federal government](/wiki/Federal_government_of_the_United_States "Federal government of the United States") responded by proposing the use of the term "[Native American](/wiki/Native_Americans_in_the_United_States "Native Americans in the United States")", to recognize the primacy of Indigenous peoples' tenure in the nation.[[71]](#cite_note-72) As may be expected among people of over 400 different cultures in the US alone, not all of the people intended to be described by this term have agreed on its use or adopted it. No single group naming convention has been accepted by all Indigenous peoples in the Americas. Most prefer to be addressed as people of their tribe or nations when not speaking about Native Americans/American Indians as a whole.[[72]](#cite_note-Mann2-73)

Since the 1970s, the word "Indigenous", which is capitalized when referring to people, has gradually emerged as a favored umbrella term. The capitalization is to acknowledge that Indigenous peoples have cultures and societies that are equal to Europeans, Africans, and Asians.[[68]](#cite_note-InTermGuide-69)[[73]](#cite_note-74) This has recently been acknowledged in the *[AP Stylebook](/wiki/AP_Stylebook "AP Stylebook")*.[[74]](#cite_note-75) Some consider it improper to refer to Indigenous people as "Indigenous Americans" or to append any colonial nationality to the term because Indigenous cultures existed before European colonization. Indigenous groups have territorial claims that are different from modern national and international borders, and when labeled as part of a country, their traditional lands are not acknowledged. Some who have written guidelines consider it more appropriate to describe an Indigenous person as "living in" or "of" the Americas, rather than calling them "American"; or simply calling them "Indigenous" without any addition of a colonial state.[[75]](#cite_note-76)[[76]](#cite_note-77)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Indigenous_peoples_of_the_Americas&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: History")]

### Peopling of the Americas

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Indigenous_peoples_of_the_Americas&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Peopling of the Americas")]

This section is an excerpt from [Peopling of the Americas](/wiki/Peopling_of_the_Americas "Peopling of the Americas").[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Peopling_of_the_Americas&action=edit)]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8b/Early_migrations_mercator.svg/500px-Early_migrations_mercator.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Early_migrations_mercator.svg)

Map of [early human migrations](/wiki/Early_human_migrations "Early human migrations") based on the [Out of Africa theory](/wiki/Recent_African_origin_of_modern_humans "Recent African origin of modern humans"); figures are in thousands of years ago (kya).[[77]](#cite_note-78)

It is believed that the [peopling of the Americas](/wiki/Peopling_of_the_Americas "Peopling of the Americas") began when [Paleolithic](/wiki/Upper_Paleolithic "Upper Paleolithic") [hunter-gatherers](/wiki/Hunter-gatherer "Hunter-gatherer") ([Paleo-Indians](/wiki/Paleo-Indians "Paleo-Indians")) entered [North America](/wiki/North_America "North America") from the [North Asian](/wiki/North_Asia "North Asia") [Mammoth steppe](/wiki/Mammoth_steppe "Mammoth steppe") via the [Beringia land bridge](/wiki/Beringia_land_bridge "Beringia land bridge"), which had formed between northeastern [Siberia](/wiki/Siberia "Siberia") and western [Alaska](/wiki/Alaska "Alaska") due to the lowering of [sea level](/wiki/Sea_level "Sea level") during the [Last Glacial Maximum](/wiki/Last_Glacial_Maximum "Last Glacial Maximum") (26,000 to 19,000 years ago).[[78]](#cite_note-Peopling_of_the_Americas_Smithsoniana-79) These populations expanded south of the [Laurentide Ice Sheet](/wiki/Laurentide_Ice_Sheet "Laurentide Ice Sheet"), either by sea or land, and spread rapidly southward, occupying both [North and South America](/wiki/Americas "Americas") no later than 14,000 years ago, and possibly before 20,000 years ago.[[79]](#cite_note-Peopling_of_the_Americas_FaganDurrani2016-80)[[80]](#cite_note-Peopling_of_the_Americas_Goebel-81)[[81]](#cite_note-Peopling_of_the_Americas_NYT-20180103-82)[[82]](#cite_note-Peopling_of_the_Americas_NAT-20180103-83)[[83]](#cite_note-Peopling_of_the_Americas_:2-84) The earliest populations in the Americas, before roughly 10,000 years ago, are known as [Paleo-Indians](/wiki/Paleo-Indians "Paleo-Indians"). Indigenous peoples of the Americas have been linked to Siberian populations by the distribution of [blood types](/wiki/Blood_types "Blood types"), and in [genetic composition](/wiki/Genetic_history_of_the_Indigenous_peoples_of_the_Americas "Genetic history of the Indigenous peoples of the Americas") as reflected by [molecular](/wiki/Molecule "Molecule") data, such as [DNA](/wiki/DNA "DNA").[[84]](#cite_note-Peopling_of_the_Americas_AshRobinson2011-85)[[85]](#cite_note-Peopling_of_the_Americas_Roberts2010-86)

While there is general agreement that the Americas were first settled from Asia, the pattern of migration and the place(s) of origin in Eurasia of the peoples who migrated to the Americas remain unclear.[[80]](#cite_note-Peopling_of_the_Americas_Goebel-81) The most generally accepted theory is that [Ancient Beringians](/wiki/Ancient_Beringian "Ancient Beringian") moved when sea levels were significantly lowered due to the [Quaternary glaciation](/wiki/Quaternary_glaciation "Quaternary glaciation"),[[86]](#cite_note-Peopling_of_the_Americas_Smithsonianb-87)[[87]](#cite_note-Peopling_of_the_Americas_national-88) following herds of now-extinct [Pleistocene](/wiki/Pleistocene "Pleistocene") [megafauna](/wiki/Megafauna "Megafauna") along *ice-free corridors* that stretched between the [Laurentide](/wiki/Laurentide_Ice_Sheet "Laurentide Ice Sheet") and [Cordilleran](/wiki/Cordilleran_Ice_Sheet "Cordilleran Ice Sheet") ice sheets.[[88]](#cite_note-89) Another proposed route has them migrating down the Pacific coast to [South America](/wiki/South_America "South America") as far as [Chile](/wiki/Chile "Chile"), either on foot or using [boats](/wiki/Boat#History "Boat").[[89]](#cite_note-90) Any archaeological evidence of coastal occupation during the last Ice Age would now have been covered by the [sea level rise](/wiki/Sea_level_rise "Sea level rise"), up to a hundred metres since then.[[90]](#cite_note-91)

The precise date for the peopling of the Americas is a long-standing open question. While advances in [archaeology](/wiki/Archaeology "Archaeology"), [Pleistocene](/wiki/Pleistocene "Pleistocene") [geology](/wiki/Geology "Geology"), [physical anthropology](/wiki/Physical_anthropology "Physical anthropology"), and [DNA analysis](/wiki/DNA_analysis "DNA analysis") have progressively shed more light on the subject, significant questions remain unresolved.[[91]](#cite_note-Peopling_of_the_Americas_Null_2022-92)[[92]](#cite_note-Peopling_of_the_Americas_Pauketat2012-93) The [Clovis First theory](/wiki/Clovis_First_theory "Clovis First theory") refers to the hypothesis that the [Clovis culture](/wiki/Clovis_culture "Clovis culture") represents the earliest human presence in the Americas about 13,000 years ago.[[93]](#cite_note-Peopling_of_the_Americas_Surovell-94) [Evidence of pre-Clovis cultures has accumulated](/wiki/Alternatives_to_the_Clovis_First_theory "Alternatives to the Clovis First theory") and pushed back the possible date of the first peopling of the Americas.[[94]](#cite_note-Peopling_of_the_Americas_Yasinski_2022-95)[[95]](#cite_note-Peopling_of_the_Americas_ArdeleanBecerra-Valdivia2020-96)[[96]](#cite_note-97)[[97]](#cite_note-98) Academics generally believe that humans reached North America south of the Laurentide Ice Sheet at some point between 15,000 and 20,000 years ago.[[91]](#cite_note-Peopling_of_the_Americas_Null_2022-92)[[94]](#cite_note-Peopling_of_the_Americas_Yasinski_2022-95)[[98]](#cite_note-Peopling_of_the_Americas_Wells2006-99)[[99]](#cite_note-Peopling_of_the_Americas_Relethford2017-100)[[100]](#cite_note-Peopling_of_the_Americas_Birx2010-101)[[101]](#cite_note-Peopling_of_the_Americas_KiczaHorn2016-102) Some new controversial archaeological evidence suggests the possibility that human arrival in the Americas may have occurred prior to the [Last Glacial Maximum](/wiki/Last_Glacial_Maximum "Last Glacial Maximum") more than 20,000 years ago.[[94]](#cite_note-Peopling_of_the_Americas_Yasinski_2022-95)[[102]](#cite_note-Peopling_of_the_Americas_Baisas_2022_l824-103)[[103]](#cite_note-104)[[104]](#cite_note-Peopling_of_the_Americas_Chatters-105)[[105]](#cite_note-Peopling_of_the_Americas_Gibbon,_Guy_E_1998-106)[[106]](#cite_note-107) The [Swan Point Archaeological Site](/wiki/Swan_Point_Archaeological_Site "Swan Point Archaeological Site") located in [Alaska](/wiki/Alaska "Alaska") has yielded the oldest evidence of human habitation that is not disputed, with artifacts that have radiocarbon dates of 14,000 years, indicating the site was occupied around 12,000 BCE.[[107]](#cite_note-108)[[108]](#cite_note-Peopling_of_the_Americas_r023-109)[[109]](#cite_note-Peopling_of_the_Americas_v518-110)

#### Pre-Columbian era

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Indigenous_peoples_of_the_Americas&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Pre-Columbian era")]

Main articles: [Pre-Columbian era](/wiki/Pre-Columbian_era "Pre-Columbian era") and [Archaeology of the Americas](/wiki/Archaeology_of_the_Americas "Archaeology of the Americas")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a5/Langs_N.Amer.png/250px-Langs_N.Amer.png)](/wiki/File:Langs_N.Amer.png)

Language families of Indigenous peoples in [North America](/wiki/North_America "North America") shown across present-day [Canada](/wiki/Canada "Canada"), [Greenland](/wiki/Greenland "Greenland"), the [United States](/wiki/United_States "United States"), and northern [Mexico](/wiki/Mexico "Mexico")

While technically referring to the era before [Christopher Columbus](/wiki/Christopher_Columbus "Christopher Columbus")' voyages of 1492 to 1504, in practice the term usually includes the history of Indigenous cultures until Europeans either conquered or significantly influenced them.[[110]](#cite_note-111) "Pre-Columbian" is used especially often in the context of discussing the pre-contact [Mesoamerican](/wiki/Mesoamerican "Mesoamerican") [Indigenous societies](/wiki/List_of_pre-Columbian_cultures "List of pre-Columbian cultures"): [Olmec](/wiki/Olmec "Olmec"); [Toltec](/wiki/Toltec "Toltec"); [Teotihuacano](/wiki/Teotihuacan "Teotihuacan"); [Zapotec](/wiki/Zapotec_civilization "Zapotec civilization"); [Mixtec](/wiki/Mixtec "Mixtec"); [Aztec](/wiki/Aztec "Aztec") and [Maya civilizations](/wiki/Maya_civilization "Maya civilization"); and the [complex cultures of the Andes](/wiki/Andean_civilizations "Andean civilizations"): [Inca Empire](/wiki/Inca_Empire "Inca Empire"), [Moche culture](/wiki/Moche_culture "Moche culture"), [Muisca Confederation](/wiki/Muisca_Confederation "Muisca Confederation"), and [Cañari](/wiki/Ca%C3%B1ari "Cañari").

The pre-Columbian era refers to all [period subdivisions](/wiki/Archaeology_of_the_Americas "Archaeology of the Americas") in the [history and prehistory of the Americas](/wiki/History_of_the_Americas "History of the Americas") before the appearance of significant European and African influences on the American continents, spanning the time of the original arrival in the [Upper Paleolithic](/wiki/Upper_Paleolithic "Upper Paleolithic") to [European colonization](/wiki/European_colonization_of_the_Americas "European colonization of the Americas") during the [early modern period](/wiki/Early_modern_period "Early modern period").[[111]](#cite_note-Method-112)
The [Norte Chico civilization](/wiki/Norte_Chico_civilization "Norte Chico civilization") (in present-day Peru) is one of the defining six original civilizations of the world, arising independently around the same time as that of Egypt.[[112]](#cite_note-Caral-113)[[113]](#cite_note-Dating-114) Many later pre-Columbian civilizations achieved great complexity, with hallmarks that included permanent or urban settlements, agriculture, engineering, astronomy, trade, civic and monumental architecture, and [complex societal hierarchies](/wiki/Complex_society "Complex society"). Some of these civilizations had long faded by the time of the first significant European and African arrivals (ca. late 15th–early 16th centuries), and are known only through [oral history](/wiki/Oral_history "Oral history") and through archaeological investigations. Others were contemporary with the contact and colonization period and were documented in historical accounts of the time. A few, such as the Mayan, Olmec, Mixtec, [Aztec](/wiki/Aztec_Empire "Aztec Empire"), and [Nahua peoples](/wiki/Nahua_peoples "Nahua peoples"), had their written languages and records. However, the European colonists of the time worked to eliminate non-Christian beliefs and burned many pre-Columbian written records. Only a few documents remained hidden and survived, leaving contemporary historians with glimpses of ancient culture and knowledge.

According to both Indigenous and European accounts and documents, American civilizations before and at the time of European encounter had achieved great complexity and many accomplishments.[[114]](#cite_note-115) For instance, the Aztecs built one of the largest cities in the world, [Tenochtitlan](/wiki/Tenochtitlan "Tenochtitlan") (the historical site of what would become [Mexico City](/wiki/Mexico_City "Mexico City")), with an estimated population of 200,000 for the city proper and a population of close to five million for the extended empire.[[115]](#cite_note-116) By comparison, the largest European cities in the 16th century were Constantinople and Paris with 300,000 and 200,000 inhabitants respectively.[[116]](#cite_note-117) The population in London, Madrid, and Rome hardly exceeded 50,000 people. In 1523, right around the time of the Spanish conquest, the entire population in the country of England was just under three million people.[[117]](#cite_note-118) This fact speaks to the level of sophistication, agriculture, governmental procedure, and rule of law that existed in Tenochtitlan, needed to govern over such a large citizenry. Indigenous civilizations also displayed impressive accomplishments in astronomy and mathematics, including the most accurate calendar in the world.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] The domestication of [maize](/wiki/Maize "Maize") or corn required thousands of years of selective breeding, and continued cultivation of multiple varieties was done with planning and selection, generally by women.

Inuit, Yupik, Aleut, and Indigenous [creation myths](/wiki/Creation_myth "Creation myth") tell of a variety of origins of their respective peoples. Some were "always there" or were created by gods or animals, some migrated from a specified [compass point](/wiki/Points_of_the_compass "Points of the compass"), and others came from "across the ocean".[[118]](#cite_note-119)

### European colonization

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Indigenous_peoples_of_the_Americas&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: European colonization")]

Main article: [European colonization of the Americas](/wiki/European_colonization_of_the_Americas "European colonization of the Americas")

See also: [Population history of Indigenous peoples of the Americas](/wiki/Population_history_of_Indigenous_peoples_of_the_Americas "Population history of Indigenous peoples of the Americas"), [Columbian exchange](/wiki/Columbian_exchange "Columbian exchange"), and [Society of the Spanish-Americans in the Spanish Colonial Americas](/wiki/Criollo_people#Spanish_colonial_caste_system "Criollo people")

The European colonization of the Americas fundamentally changed the lives and cultures of the resident Indigenous peoples. Although the exact pre-colonization population count of the Americas is unknown, scholars estimate that Indigenous populations diminished by between 80% and 90% during the first centuries of European colonization. Most scholars estimate a pre-colonization population of around 50 million, with other scholars arguing for an estimate of 100 million. Estimates reach as high as 145 million.[[119]](#cite_note-120)[[120]](#cite_note-121)[[121]](#cite_note-:7-122) [William Denevan](/wiki/William_Denevan "William Denevan") estimates of the pre-contact population range from 8 million to 112 million, falling to under 6 million by 1650.[[122]](#cite_note-p201-123)[*[page needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources "Wikipedia:Citing sources")*]

Epidemics ravaged the Americas with diseases, such as [smallpox](/wiki/Smallpox "Smallpox"), [measles](/wiki/Measles "Measles"), and [cholera](/wiki/Cholera "Cholera"), which the early colonists brought from Europe. The spread of infectious diseases was slow initially, as European populations were relatively small. This changed when the Europeans began the [trafficking](/wiki/Human_trafficking "Human trafficking") of massive numbers of [enslaved Western and Central African people](/wiki/Atlantic_slave_trade "Atlantic slave trade") to the Americas, drastically increasing the population. These enslaved Africans carried many of the same diseases as Europeans, such as smallpox, along with many tropical diseases unknown to both the indigenous populations and Europeans. In 1520, an African who had been infected with smallpox had arrived in Yucatán. By 1558, the disease had spread throughout South America and had arrived at the Plata basin.[[123]](#cite_note-:0-124) Colonist violence towards Indigenous peoples accelerated the loss of lives. European colonists perpetrated massacres on the Indigenous peoples and enslaved them.[[124]](#cite_note-125)[[125]](#cite_note-126)[[126]](#cite_note-127) According to the U.S. Bureau of the Census (1894), the [North American Indian Wars](/wiki/American_Indian_Wars "American Indian Wars") of the 19th century had a known death toll of about 19,000 Europeans and 30,000 Native Americans, and an estimated total death toll of 45,000 Native Americans.[[127]](#cite_note-128)

The first Indigenous group encountered by Columbus, the 250,000 [Taínos](/wiki/Ta%C3%ADno "Taíno") of [Hispaniola](/wiki/Hispaniola "Hispaniola"), represented the dominant culture in the [Greater Antilles](/wiki/Greater_Antilles "Greater Antilles") and the Bahamas. Within thirty years about 70% of the Taínos had died.[[128]](#cite_note-Histoire-129) They had no immunity to European diseases, so outbreaks of [measles](/wiki/Measles "Measles") and [smallpox](/wiki/Smallpox "Smallpox") ravaged their population.[[129]](#cite_note-130) One such outbreak occurred in a camp of enslaved Africans, where smallpox spread to the nearby [Taíno](/wiki/Ta%C3%ADno "Taíno") population and reduced their numbers by 50%.[[123]](#cite_note-:0-124) Increasing punishment of the Taínos for revolting against forced labor, despite measures put in place by the [encomienda](/wiki/Encomienda "Encomienda"), which included religious education and protection from warring tribes,[[130]](#cite_note-131) eventually led to the last great [Taíno rebellion](/wiki/Ta%C3%ADno_rebellion "Taíno rebellion") (1511–1529).

Following years of mistreatment, the Taínos began to adopt suicidal behaviors, with women aborting or killing their infants and men jumping from cliffs or ingesting untreated [cassava](/wiki/Cassava "Cassava"), a violent poison.[[128]](#cite_note-Histoire-129) Eventually, a Taíno Cacique named [Enriquillo](/wiki/Enriquillo "Enriquillo") managed to hold out in the [Baoruco Mountain Range](/wiki/Baoruco_Mountain_Range "Baoruco Mountain Range") for thirteen years, causing serious damage to the Spanish, Carib-held plantations and their [Indian auxiliaries](/wiki/Indian_auxiliaries "Indian auxiliaries").[[131]](#cite_note-132)[*[failed verification](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability "Wikipedia:Verifiability")*] Hearing of the seriousness of the revolt, [Emperor Charles V](/wiki/Charles_V,_Holy_Roman_Emperor "Charles V, Holy Roman Emperor") (also King of Spain) sent Captain Francisco Barrionuevo to negotiate a peace treaty with the ever-increasing number of rebels. Two months later, after consultation with the Audencia of Santo Domingo, Enriquillo was offered any part of the island to live in peace.

The [Laws of Burgos, 1512–1513](/wiki/Laws_of_Burgos "Laws of Burgos"), were the first codified set of laws governing the behavior of Spanish settlers in America, particularly concerning Indigenous peoples. The laws forbade the maltreatment of them and endorsed their conversion to [Catholicism](/wiki/Catholicism "Catholicism").[[132]](#cite_note-133) The Spanish crown found it difficult to enforce these laws in distant colonies.

Epidemic disease was the overwhelming cause of the [population decline](/wiki/Population_decline "Population decline") of the Indigenous peoples.[[133]](#cite_note-134)[[134]](#cite_note-135) After initial contact with Europeans and Africans, [Old World](/wiki/Old_World "Old World") diseases caused the deaths of 90 to 95% of the native population of the [New World](/wiki/New_World "New World") in the following 150 years.[[135]](#cite_note-136) [Smallpox](/wiki/Smallpox "Smallpox") killed from one-third to half of the native population of Hispaniola in 1518.[[136]](#cite_note-137)[[137]](#cite_note-138) By killing the Incan ruler [Huayna Capac](/wiki/Huayna_Capac "Huayna Capac"), smallpox caused the [Inca Civil War](/wiki/Inca_Civil_War "Inca Civil War") of 1529–1532. Smallpox was only the first epidemic. [Typhus](/wiki/Typhus "Typhus") (probably) in 1546, [influenza](/wiki/Influenza "Influenza") and smallpox together in 1558, smallpox again in 1589, [diphtheria](/wiki/Diphtheria "Diphtheria") in 1614, and [measles](/wiki/Measles "Measles") in 1618—all ravaged the remains of Inca culture.

Smallpox killed millions of native inhabitants of Mexico.[[138]](#cite_note-139)[[139]](#cite_note-140) Unintentionally introduced at Veracruz with the arrival of [Pánfilo de Narváez](/wiki/P%C3%A1nfilo_de_Narv%C3%A1ez "Pánfilo de Narváez") on 23 April 1520, smallpox ravaged Mexico in the 1520s,[[140]](#cite_note-141) possibly killing over 150,000 in Tenochtitlán (the heartland of the Aztec Empire) alone, and aiding in the victory of [Hernán Cortés](/wiki/Hern%C3%A1n_Cort%C3%A9s "Hernán Cortés") over the [Aztec Empire](/wiki/Aztec_Empire "Aztec Empire") at Tenochtitlan (present-day Mexico City) in 1521.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*][[123]](#cite_note-:0-124)

There are many factors as to why Indigenous peoples suffered such immense losses from Afro-Eurasian diseases. Many Old World diseases, like cow pox, are acquired from domesticated animals that are not indigenous to the Americas. European populations had adapted to these diseases, and built up resistance, over many generations. Many of the Old World diseases that were brought over to the Americas were diseases, like [yellow fever](/wiki/Yellow_fever "Yellow fever"), that were relatively manageable if infected as a child, but were deadly if infected as an adult. Children could often survive the disease, resulting in immunity to the disease for the rest of their lives. But contact with the disease by adults without this childhood or inherited immunity often proved fatal.[[123]](#cite_note-:0-124)[[141]](#cite_note-142)

Colonization of the Caribbean led to the destruction of the [Arawaks](/wiki/Arawak "Arawak") of the [Lesser Antilles](/wiki/Lesser_Antilles "Lesser Antilles"). Their culture was destroyed by 1650. Only 500 had survived by the year 1550, though the bloodlines continued through to the modern populace. In Amazonia, Indigenous societies weathered centuries of colonization and genocide.[[142]](#cite_note-143)

Contact with European diseases such as smallpox and measles killed between 50 and 67 percent of the Indigenous population of North America in the first hundred years after the arrival of Europeans.[[143]](#cite_note-144) Some 90 percent of the native population near [Massachusetts Bay Colony](/wiki/Massachusetts_Bay_Colony "Massachusetts Bay Colony") died of smallpox in an epidemic in 1617–1619.[[144]](#cite_note-145) In 1633, in [Fort Orange (New Netherland)](/wiki/Fort_Orange_(New_Netherland) "Fort Orange (New Netherland)"), the Native Americans there were exposed to smallpox because of contact with Europeans. As it had done elsewhere, the virus wiped out entire population groups of Native Americans.[[145]](#cite_note-146) It reached [Lake Ontario](/wiki/Lake_Ontario "Lake Ontario") in 1636, and the lands of the [Iroquois](/wiki/Iroquois "Iroquois") by 1679.[[146]](#cite_note-147)[[147]](#cite_note-148) During the 1770s smallpox killed at least 30% of the [West Coast](/wiki/West_Coast_of_the_United_States "West Coast of the United States") Native Americans.[[148]](#cite_note-149) The [1775–82 North American smallpox epidemic](/wiki/1775%E2%80%9382_North_American_smallpox_epidemic "1775–82 North American smallpox epidemic") and the [1837 Great Plains smallpox epidemic](/wiki/1837_Great_Plains_smallpox_epidemic "1837 Great Plains smallpox epidemic") brought devastation and drastic population depletion among the [Plains Indians](/wiki/Plains_Indians "Plains Indians").[[149]](#cite_note-150)[[150]](#cite_note-151) In 1832 the federal government of the United States established a [smallpox vaccination](/wiki/Smallpox_vaccine "Smallpox vaccine") program for Native Americans (*The Indian Vaccination Act of 1832*).[[151]](#cite_note-152)

The [Indigenous peoples in Brazil](/wiki/Indigenous_peoples_in_Brazil "Indigenous peoples in Brazil") declined from a pre-Columbian high of an estimated three million[[152]](#cite_note-153) to some 300,000 in 1997.[*[dubious](/wiki/Wikipedia:Accuracy_dispute#Disputed_statement "Wikipedia:Accuracy dispute") – [discuss](/wiki/Talk:Indigenous_peoples_of_the_Americas#Dubious "Talk:Indigenous peoples of the Americas")*][*[failed verification](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability "Wikipedia:Verifiability")*][[153]](#cite_note-Brazil_urged_to_protect_Indians-154)

The [Spanish Empire](/wiki/Spanish_Empire "Spanish Empire") and other Europeans re-introduced [horses](/wiki/Horse "Horse") to the Americas. Some of these animals escaped and began to breed and increase their numbers in the wild.[[154]](#cite_note-155) The reintroduction of the [horse](/wiki/Horse "Horse"), extinct in the Americas for over 7500 years, had a profound impact on [Indigenous cultures](/wiki/Horse_culture "Horse culture") in several regions, such as those of the [Great Plains](/wiki/Plains_Indians "Plains Indians"), the [Northwest Plateau](/wiki/Indigenous_peoples_of_the_Northwest_Plateau "Indigenous peoples of the Northwest Plateau"), the [Great Basin](/wiki/Indigenous_peoples_of_the_Great_Basin "Indigenous peoples of the Great Basin"), [Aridoamerica](/wiki/Aridoamerica "Aridoamerica"), the [Gran Chaco](/wiki/Gran_Chaco_people "Gran Chaco people") and the [Southern Cone](/wiki/Southern_Cone "Southern Cone"). By domesticating horses, some tribes had great success: horses enabled them to expand their territories, exchange more goods with neighboring tribes, and more easily capture [game](/wiki/Game_(hunting) "Game (hunting)"), such as [bison](/wiki/Bison "Bison").

According to Erin McKenna and Scott L. Pratt, the Indigenous population of the Americas was 145 million in the late 15th and by the late 17th century, had been reduced to 15 million due to [epidemics](/wiki/Native_American_disease_and_epidemics "Native American disease and epidemics"), wars, massacres, [mass rapes](/wiki/Mass_rapes "Mass rapes"), starvation, and enslavement.[[121]](#cite_note-:7-122)

## Indigenous historical trauma

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Indigenous_peoples_of_the_Americas&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Indigenous historical trauma")]

See also: [Historical trauma § Indigenous historical trauma](/wiki/Historical_trauma#Indigenous_historical_trauma "Historical trauma")

![Map](https://maps.wikimedia.org/img/osm-intl,a,a,a,300x200.png?lang=en&domain=en.wikipedia.org&title=Indigenous_peoples_of_the_Americas&revid=1343524062&groups=_089419b02541f8672fd8ffe383f84dfabe489144&parser=legacy)

[Data](https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/Data:Canadian_Indian_residential_school_gravesites.map "commons:Data:Canadian Indian residential school gravesites.map")

Indigenous historical trauma (IHT) is the trauma that can accumulate across generations and develop as a result of the historical ramifications of [colonization](/wiki/Colonization "Colonization") and is linked to mental and physical health hardships and population decline.[[155]](#cite_note-:1-156) IHT affects many different people in a multitude of ways because the Indigenous community and their history are diverse.

Many studies (such as Whitbeck et al., 2014;[[156]](#cite_note-157) Brockie, 2012; Anastasio et al., 2016;[[157]](#cite_note-:2-158) Clark & Winterowd, 2012;[[158]](#cite_note-:3-159) Tucker et al., 2016)[[159]](#cite_note-:4-160) have evaluated the impact of IHT on health outcomes of Indigenous communities from the United States and Canada. IHT is a difficult term to standardize and measure because of the vast and variable diversity of Indigenous people and their communities. Therefore, it is an arduous task to assign an [operational definition](/wiki/Operational_definition "Operational definition") and systematically collect data when studying IHT. Many of the studies that incorporate IHT measure it in different ways, making it hard to compile data and review it holistically. This is an important point that provides context for the following studies that attempt to understand the relationship between IHT and potential adverse health impacts.

Some of the methodologies to measure IHT include a "Historical Losses Scale" (HLS), "Historical Losses Associated Symptoms Scale" (HLASS), and residential school ancestry studies.[[155]](#cite_note-:1-156): 23 HLS uses a survey format that includes "12 kinds of historical losses", such as loss of language and loss of land and asks participants how often they think about those losses.[[155]](#cite_note-:1-156): 23 The HLASS includes 12 emotional reactions, and asks participants how they feel when they think about these losses.[[155]](#cite_note-:1-156) Lastly, the [residential school](/wiki/Canadian_Indian_residential_school_system "Canadian Indian residential school system") ancestry studies ask respondents if their parents, grandparents, great-grandparents, or "elders from their community" went to a residential school to understand if family or community history in residential schools is associated with negative health outcomes.[[155]](#cite_note-:1-156): 25 In a comprehensive review of the research literature, Joseph Gone and colleagues[[155]](#cite_note-:1-156) compiled and compared outcomes for studies using these IHT measures relative to the health outcomes of Indigenous peoples. The study defined negative health outcomes to include such concepts as [anxiety](/wiki/Anxiety "Anxiety"), [suicidal ideation](/wiki/Suicidal_ideation "Suicidal ideation"), [suicide attempts](/wiki/Suicide_attempt "Suicide attempt"), [polysubstance abuse](/wiki/Polysubstance_dependence "Polysubstance dependence"), [PTSD](/wiki/Post-traumatic_stress_disorder "Post-traumatic stress disorder"), [depression](/wiki/Depression_(mood) "Depression (mood)"), [binge eating](/wiki/Binge_eating "Binge eating"), anger, and sexual abuse.[[155]](#cite_note-:1-156)

The connection between IHT and health conditions is complicated because of the difficult nature of measuring IHT, the unknown directionality of IHT and health outcomes, and because the term *Indigenous people* used in the various samples comprises a huge population of individuals with drastically different experiences and histories. That being said some studies such as Bombay, Matheson, and Anisman (2014),[[160]](#cite_note-161) Elias et al. (2012),[[161]](#cite_note-162) and Pearce et al. (2008)[[162]](#cite_note-163) found that Indigenous respondents with a connection to residential schools have more negative health outcomes (e.g., suicide ideation, suicide attempts, and depression) than those who did not have a connection to residential schools. Additionally, Indigenous respondents with higher HLS and HLASS scores had one or more negative health outcomes.[[155]](#cite_note-:1-156) While there are many studies[[157]](#cite_note-:2-158)[[163]](#cite_note-164)[[158]](#cite_note-:3-159)[[164]](#cite_note-165)[[159]](#cite_note-:4-160) that found an association between IHT and adverse health outcomes, scholars continue to suggest that it remains difficult to understand the impact of IHT. IHT needs to be systematically measured. Indigenous people also need to be understood in separate categories based on similar experiences, location, and background as opposed to being categorized as one monolithic group.[[155]](#cite_note-:1-156)

### Plants

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Indigenous_peoples_of_the_Americas&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Plants")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f0/Museo_Nacional_de_Antropolog%C3%ADa_-_MA%C3%8DZ.jpg/250px-Museo_Nacional_de_Antropolog%C3%ADa_-_MA%C3%8DZ.jpg)](/wiki/File:Museo_Nacional_de_Antropolog%C3%ADa_-_MA%C3%8DZ.jpg)

The ancient [Mesoamerican](/wiki/Mesoamerican "Mesoamerican") engraving of [maize](/wiki/Maize "Maize") now on display at the [National Museum of Anthropology](/wiki/National_Museum_of_Anthropology_(Mexico) "National Museum of Anthropology (Mexico)") in Mexico

For thousands of years, Indigenous peoples domesticated, bred, and cultivated a large array of plant species. These species now constitute between 50% and 60% of all crops in cultivation worldwide.[[165]](#cite_note-166) In certain cases, the Indigenous peoples developed entirely new species and strains through [artificial selection](/wiki/Artificial_selection "Artificial selection"), as with the domestication and breeding of [maize](/wiki/Maize "Maize") from wild *[teosinte](/wiki/Teosinte "Teosinte")* grasses in the valleys of southern Mexico. Numerous such agricultural products retain their Native names in the English and Spanish lexicons.

The South American highlands became a center of early agriculture. Genetic testing of the wide variety of [cultivars](/wiki/Cultivar "Cultivar") and wild species suggests that the [potato](/wiki/Potato "Potato") has a single origin in the area of southern [Peru](/wiki/Peru "Peru"),[[166]](#cite_note-167) from a species in the *[Solanum brevicaule](/wiki/Solanum_brevicaule "Solanum brevicaule")* complex. Over 99% of all modern cultivated potatoes worldwide are descendants of a subspecies indigenous to south-central [Chile](/wiki/Chile "Chile"),[[167]](#cite_note-chile-168) *Solanum tuberosum ssp. tuberosum*, where it was cultivated as long as 10,000 years ago.[[168]](#cite_note-Solis_2007_0-169)[[169]](#cite_note-John_Michael_Francis_2005-170) According to [Linda Newson](/wiki/Linda_Newson "Linda Newson"), "It is clear that in pre-Columbian times some groups struggled to survive and often suffered food shortages and [famines](/wiki/Famine "Famine"), while others enjoyed a varied and substantial diet."[[170]](#cite_note-171)

Persistent drought around AD 850 coincided with the collapse of the [Classic Maya](/wiki/Classic_Maya_collapse "Classic Maya collapse") civilization, and the famine of One Rabbit (AD 1454) was a major catastrophe in Mexico.[[171]](#cite_note-172)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e7/Phaseolus_vulgaris_seed.jpg/250px-Phaseolus_vulgaris_seed.jpg)](/wiki/File:Phaseolus_vulgaris_seed.jpg)

The [common bean](/wiki/Phaseolus_vulgaris "Phaseolus vulgaris") is native to Mexico and Central America and later began to be cultivated in South America.

Indigenous peoples of North America began practicing [farming](/wiki/Farming "Farming") approximately 4,000 years ago, late in the [Archaic](/wiki/Archaic_period_in_the_Americas "Archaic period in the Americas") period of North American cultures. Technology had advanced to the point where pottery had started to become common and the small-scale felling of trees had become feasible. Concurrently, the Archaic [Indigenous peoples began using fire](/wiki/Native_American_use_of_fire "Native American use of fire") in a controlled manner. They carried out the intentional burning of vegetation to mimic the effects of natural fires that tended to clear forest understories. It made travel easier and facilitated the growth of herbs and berry-producing plants, which were important both for food and for medicines.[[172]](#cite_note-Owen2002-173)

In the [Mississippi River](/wiki/Mississippi_River "Mississippi River") valley, Europeans noted that Native Americans managed groves of [nut](/wiki/Nut_(fruit) "Nut (fruit)") and [fruit trees](/wiki/Fruit_tree "Fruit tree") not far from villages and towns and their gardens and agricultural fields. They would have used [prescribed burning](/wiki/Controlled_burn "Controlled burn") farther away, in forest and prairie areas.[[173]](#cite_note-Lentz2000-174)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a2/Tomato.jpg/250px-Tomato.jpg)](/wiki/File:Tomato.jpg)

The tomato (jitomate, in central Mexico) was later cultivated by the pre-Hispanic civilizations of Mexico.

Many crops first domesticated by Indigenous peoples are now produced and used globally, most notably [maize](/wiki/Maize "Maize") (or "corn") arguably the most important crop in the world.[[174]](#cite_note-175) Other significant crops include [cassava](/wiki/Cassava "Cassava"); [chia](/wiki/Salvia_hispanica "Salvia hispanica"); [squash](/wiki/Cucurbita "Cucurbita") (pumpkins, zucchini, [marrow](/wiki/Marrow_(vegetable) "Marrow (vegetable)"), [acorn squash](/wiki/Acorn_squash "Acorn squash"), [butternut squash](/wiki/Butternut_squash "Butternut squash")); the [pinto bean](/wiki/Pinto_bean "Pinto bean"), *[Phaseolus](/wiki/Phaseolus "Phaseolus")* beans including most [common beans](/wiki/Common_bean "Common bean"), [tepary beans](/wiki/Phaseolus_acutifolius "Phaseolus acutifolius"), and [lima beans](/wiki/Lima_bean "Lima bean"); [tomatoes](/wiki/Tomato "Tomato"); [potatoes](/wiki/Potato "Potato"); [sweet potatoes](/wiki/Sweet_potato "Sweet potato"); [avocados](/wiki/Avocado "Avocado"); [peanuts](/wiki/Peanut "Peanut"); [cocoa beans](/wiki/Cocoa_bean "Cocoa bean") (used to make [chocolate](/wiki/Chocolate "Chocolate")); [vanilla](/wiki/Vanilla "Vanilla"); [strawberries](/wiki/Strawberries "Strawberries"); [pineapples](/wiki/Pineapple "Pineapple"); [peppers](/wiki/Capsicum "Capsicum") (species and varieties of *[Capsicum](/wiki/Capsicum "Capsicum")*, including [bell peppers](/wiki/Bell_pepper "Bell pepper"), [jalapeños](/wiki/Jalape%C3%B1o "Jalapeño"), [paprika](/wiki/Paprika "Paprika"), and [chili peppers](/wiki/Chili_pepper "Chili pepper")); [sunflower seeds](/wiki/Common_sunflower "Common sunflower"); [rubber](/wiki/Natural_rubber "Natural rubber"); [brazilwood](/wiki/Paubrasilia "Paubrasilia"); [chicle](/wiki/Chicle "Chicle"); [tobacco](/wiki/Tobacco "Tobacco"); [coca](/wiki/Coca "Coca"); [blueberries](/wiki/Blueberry "Blueberry"), [cranberries](/wiki/Cranberry "Cranberry"), and some species of [cotton](/wiki/Cotton "Cotton").

Studies of contemporary Indigenous environmental management—including agro-forestry practices among [Itza](/wiki/Itza_people "Itza people") [Maya](/wiki/Maya_peoples "Maya peoples") in Guatemala and hunting and fishing among the [Menominee](/wiki/Menominee "Menominee") of Wisconsin—suggest that longstanding "sacred values" may represent a summary of sustainable millennial traditions.[[175]](#cite_note-176)

### Animals

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Indigenous_peoples_of_the_Americas&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Animals")]

Numerous [Native American dog breeds](/wiki/Native_American_dogs "Native American dogs") have been used by the people of the Americas, such as the [Canadian Eskimo dog](/wiki/Canadian_Eskimo_Dog "Canadian Eskimo Dog"), the [Carolina dog](/wiki/Carolina_Dog "Carolina Dog"), and the [Chihuahua](/wiki/Chihuahua_(dog_breed) "Chihuahua (dog breed)"). Some [Indigenous peoples in the Great Plains](/wiki/Plains_Indians "Plains Indians") used dogs for pulling [travois](/wiki/Travois "Travois"), while others like the [Tahltan bear dog](/wiki/Tahltan_Bear_Dog "Tahltan Bear Dog") were bred to hunt larger game. Some Andean cultures also bred the [Chiribaya](/wiki/Chiribaya_Dog "Chiribaya Dog") to herd [llamas](/wiki/Llama "Llama"). The vast majority of indigenous dog breeds in the Americas went extinct, due to being replaced by dogs of European origin.[[176]](#cite_note-leathlobhair2018-177)

The [Fuegian dog](/wiki/Fuegian_dog "Fuegian dog") was a domesticated variation of the [culpeo](/wiki/Culpeo "Culpeo") that was raised by [several cultures in Tierra del Fuego](/wiki/Fuegians "Fuegians"), like the [Selkʼnam](/wiki/Selk%CA%BCnam_people "Selkʼnam people") and the [Yahgan](/wiki/Yahgan_people "Yahgan people").[[177]](#cite_note-Petrigh_&_Fugassa_2013-178) It was exterminated by Argentine and Chilean settlers during the [Selknam genocide](/wiki/Selknam_genocide "Selknam genocide").[[178]](#cite_note-179)

Several bird species, such as [turkeys](/wiki/Domestic_turkey "Domestic turkey"), [Muscovy ducks](/wiki/Muscovy_duck "Muscovy duck"), [Puna ibis](/wiki/Puna_ibis "Puna ibis"), and [neotropic cormorants](/wiki/Neotropic_cormorant "Neotropic cormorant") were domesticated by various peoples in Mesoamerica and South America to be used for poultry.

In the Andes, Indigenous peoples domesticated llamas and [alpacas](/wiki/Alpaca "Alpaca") to produce fiber and meat. The llama was the only [beast of burden](/wiki/Pack_animal "Pack animal") besides the dog in the Americas before European colonization.

[Guinea pigs](/wiki/Guinea_pig "Guinea pig") were domesticated from wild [cavies](/wiki/Cavia "Cavia") to be raised for meat consumption in the Andean region. Guinea pigs are now widely raised in Western society as household pets.

In [Oasisamerica](/wiki/Oasisamerica "Oasisamerica"), several cultures raised [scarlet macaws](/wiki/Scarlet_macaws "Scarlet macaws") imported from [Mesoamerica](/wiki/Mesoamerica "Mesoamerica") for their feathers.[[179]](#cite_note-180)[[180]](#cite_note-181)

In the [Maya civilization](/wiki/Maya_civilization "Maya civilization"), [stingless bees](/wiki/Stingless_bee "Stingless bee") were [domesticated](/wiki/Beekeeping "Beekeeping") to produce [balché](/wiki/Balch%C3%A9 "Balché").[[181]](#cite_note-182)

[Cochineal](/wiki/Cochineal "Cochineal") were harvested by Mesoamerican and Andean civilizations for coloring fabrics via [carminic acid](/wiki/Carminic_acid "Carminic acid").[[182]](#cite_note-Phipps-183)[[183]](#cite_note-Pearlstein-184)[[184]](#cite_note-tym-185)

## Culture

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Indigenous_peoples_of_the_Americas&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Culture")]

Further information: [Classification of Indigenous peoples of the Americas](/wiki/Classification_of_Indigenous_peoples_of_the_Americas "Classification of Indigenous peoples of the Americas"), [Mythologies of the Indigenous peoples of the Americas](/wiki/Mythologies_of_the_Indigenous_peoples_of_the_Americas "Mythologies of the Indigenous peoples of the Americas"), [Category:Archaeological cultures of North America](/wiki/Category:Archaeological_cultures_of_North_America "Category:Archaeological cultures of North America"), and [Category:Archaeological cultures of South America](/wiki/Category:Archaeological_cultures_of_South_America "Category:Archaeological cultures of South America")

Cultural practices in the Americas seem to have been shared mostly within geographical zones where distinct ethnic groups adopt shared cultural traits, similar technologies, and social organizations. An example of such a [cultural area](/wiki/Cultural_area "Cultural area") is [Mesoamerica](/wiki/Mesoamerica "Mesoamerica"), where millennia of coexistence and shared development among the peoples of the region produced a fairly homogeneous culture with complex agricultural and social patterns. Another well-known example is the North American plains where until the 19th century several peoples shared the traits of [nomadic](/wiki/Nomad "Nomad") hunter-gatherers based primarily on bison hunting.

### Languages

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Indigenous_peoples_of_the_Americas&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Languages")]

Main article: [Indigenous languages of the Americas](/wiki/Indigenous_languages_of_the_Americas "Indigenous languages of the Americas")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/45/SouthAmerican_families.png/250px-SouthAmerican_families.png)](/wiki/File:SouthAmerican_families.png)

The major indigenous [language families](/wiki/Indigenous_languages_of_South_America "Indigenous languages of South America") of much of present-day [South America](/wiki/South_America "South America") and [Panama](/wiki/Panama "Panama")

Indigenous languages in North America have been classified into 56 groups or stock tongues, in which the spoken languages of the various nations may be said to center. In connection with speech, reference may be made to gesture language which was highly developed in parts of this area. Of equal interest is the picture writing especially well developed among the [Anishinaabe](/wiki/Ojibwe_language "Ojibwe language") and [Lenape](/wiki/Lenape "Lenape") nations.[[185]](#cite_note-186)

### Writing systems

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Indigenous_peoples_of_the_Americas&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Writing systems")]

See also: [Syllabics used by Indigenous peoples living in Canada](/wiki/Canadian_Aboriginal_syllabics "Canadian Aboriginal syllabics"), [Cherokee syllabary](/wiki/Cherokee_syllabary "Cherokee syllabary"), and [Quipu](/wiki/Quipu "Quipu")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/05/Palenque_glyphs-edit1.jpg/250px-Palenque_glyphs-edit1.jpg)](/wiki/File:Palenque_glyphs-edit1.jpg)

[Maya glyphs](/wiki/Maya_script "Maya script") in stucco now on display at Museo de sitio in [Palenque](/wiki/Palenque "Palenque"), Mexico

Beginning in the 1st millennium BCE, pre-Columbian cultures in [Mesoamerica](/wiki/Mesoamerica "Mesoamerica") developed several [Indigenous writing systems](/wiki/Mesoamerican_writing_systems "Mesoamerican writing systems") (independent of any influence from the writing systems that existed in other parts of the world). The [Cascajal Block](/wiki/Cascajal_Block "Cascajal Block") is perhaps the earliest-known example in the Americas of what may be an extensive written text. The [Olmec](/wiki/Olmec "Olmec") hieroglyphs tablet has been indirectly dated (from ceramic shards found in the same context) to approximately 900 BCE which is around the same time that the Olmec occupation of [San Lorenzo Tenochtitlán](/wiki/San_Lorenzo_Tenochtitl%C3%A1n "San Lorenzo Tenochtitlán") began to weaken.[[186]](#cite_note-187)

The [Maya writing system](/wiki/Maya_script "Maya script") was [logosyllabic](/wiki/Logosyllabic "Logosyllabic") (a combination of [phonetic](/wiki/Phonetic "Phonetic") [syllabic](/wiki/Syllabary "Syllabary") symbols and [logograms](/wiki/Logogram "Logogram")). It is the only pre-Columbian writing system known to have completely represented the [spoken language](/wiki/Spoken_language "Spoken language") of its community. It has more than a thousand different [glyphs](/wiki/Glyph "Glyph"), but a few are variations on the same sign or have the same meaning, many appear only rarely or in particular localities, no more than about five hundred were in use in any given time, and, of those, it seems only about two hundred (including variations) represented a particular phoneme or syllable.[[187]](#cite_note-188)[*[page needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources "Wikipedia:Citing sources")*][[188]](#cite_note-189)[*[page needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources "Wikipedia:Citing sources")*][[189]](#cite_note-190)

The [Zapotec writing system](/wiki/Zapotec_script "Zapotec script"), one of the earliest in the Americas,[[190]](#cite_note-ReferenceA-191) was [logographic](/wiki/Logogram "Logogram") and presumably [syllabic](/wiki/Syllabary "Syllabary").[[190]](#cite_note-ReferenceA-191) There are remnants of Zapotec writing in inscriptions on some of the monumental architecture of the period, but so few inscriptions are extant that it is difficult to fully describe the writing system. The oldest example of the Zapotec script, dating from around 600 BCE, is on a monument that was discovered in [San José Mogote](/wiki/San_Jos%C3%A9_Mogote "San José Mogote").[[191]](#cite_note-192)[*[full citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources#What_information_to_include "Wikipedia:Citing sources")*]

[Aztec codices](/wiki/Aztec_codices "Aztec codices") (singular *[codex](/wiki/Codex "Codex")*) are books that were written by pre-Columbian and colonial-era [Aztecs](/wiki/Aztec "Aztec"). These codices are some of the best [primary sources](/wiki/Primary_sources "Primary sources") for descriptions of [Aztec culture](/wiki/Aztec_society "Aztec society"). The pre-Columbian codices are largely pictorial; they do not contain symbols that represent spoken or written language.[[192]](#cite_note-193) By contrast, colonial-era codices contain not only [Aztec pictograms](/wiki/Aztec_writing "Aztec writing"), but also writing that uses the [Latin alphabet](/wiki/Latin_alphabet "Latin alphabet") in several languages: [Classical Nahuatl](/wiki/Classical_Nahuatl "Classical Nahuatl"), Spanish, and occasionally [Latin](/wiki/Latin "Latin").

Spanish mendicants in the sixteenth century taught Indigenous scribes in their communities to write their languages using Latin letters, and there are a large number of local-level documents in [Nahuatl](/wiki/Nahuatl "Nahuatl"), [Zapotec](/wiki/Zapotecan_languages "Zapotecan languages"), [Mixtec](/wiki/Mixtec "Mixtec"), and Yucatec [Maya](/wiki/Mayan_languages "Mayan languages") from the colonial era, many of which were part of lawsuits and other legal matters. Although Spaniards initially taught Indigenous scribes alphabetic writing, the tradition became self-perpetuating at the local level.[[193]](#cite_note-194) The Spanish crown gathered such documentation, and contemporary Spanish translations were made for legal cases. Scholars have translated and analyzed these documents in what is called the [New Philology](/wiki/New_Philology_(Latin_America) "New Philology (Latin America)") to write histories of Indigenous peoples from Indigenous viewpoints.[[194]](#cite_note-195)

The [Wiigwaasabak](/wiki/Wiigwaasabak "Wiigwaasabak"), [birch bark](/wiki/Birch_bark "Birch bark") scrolls on which the [Ojibwa](/wiki/Ojibwa "Ojibwa"), an [Anishinaabe](/wiki/Anishinaabe "Anishinaabe")) people, wrote complex geometrical patterns and shapes, can also be considered a form of writing, as can [Mi'kmaq hieroglyphics](/wiki/Mi%27kmaq_hieroglyphic_writing "Mi'kmaq hieroglyphic writing").

Aboriginal syllabic writing, or simply syllabics, is a family of [abugidas](/wiki/Abugida "Abugida") used to write some Indigenous languages of the [Algonquian](/wiki/Algonquian_languages "Algonquian languages"), [Inuit](/wiki/Eskimo%E2%80%93Aleut_languages "Eskimo–Aleut languages"), and [Athabaskan](/wiki/Athabaskan_languages "Athabaskan languages") language families.

### Music and art

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Indigenous_peoples_of_the_Americas&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Music and art")]

Main articles: [Visual arts by Indigenous peoples of the Americas](/wiki/Visual_arts_by_Indigenous_peoples_of_the_Americas "Visual arts by Indigenous peoples of the Americas") and [Indigenous music](/wiki/Native_American_music "Native American music")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/53/JuPi_1.jpg/250px-JuPi_1.jpg)](/wiki/File:JuPi_1.jpg)

Indigenous peoples textile art in 1995 by Julia Pingushat, including [Inuk](/wiki/Inuk "Inuk"), [Arviat](/wiki/Arviat "Arviat"), Nunavut, Canada, wool, and embroidery floss

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/68/Gorget%2C_Peru%2C_north_coast%2C_Chimu_style%2C_c._1350-1450_AD%2C_feathers%2C_reeds%2C_copper_bars%2C_silver%2C_metal_overlaid_with_skin_-_Textile_Museum%2C_George_Washington_University_-_DSC09898.JPG/250px-thumbnail.jpg)](/wiki/File:Gorget,_Peru,_north_coast,_Chimu_style,_c._1350-1450_AD,_feathers,_reeds,_copper_bars,_silver,_metal_overlaid_with_skin_-_Textile_Museum,_George_Washington_University_-_DSC09898.JPG)

[Chimu culture](/wiki/Chimu_culture "Chimu culture") feather pectoral, feathers, reed, copper, silver, hide, cordage, c. 1350–1450

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/31/Pan_flute_played_2.jpg/250px-Pan_flute_played_2.jpg)](/wiki/File:Pan_flute_played_2.jpg)

An Indigenous man playing a panpipe, antara, or siku

[Indigenous music](/wiki/Native_American_music "Native American music") can vary between cultures, however, there are significant commonalities. Traditional music often centers around [drumming](/wiki/Drum "Drum") and singing. [Rattles](/wiki/Rattle_(percussion_instrument) "Rattle (percussion instrument)"), [clapper sticks](/wiki/Clapper_stick "Clapper stick"), and rasps are also popular percussive instruments, both historically and in contemporary cultures. [Flutes](/wiki/Flute "Flute") are made of river cane, cedar, and other woods. The [Apache](/wiki/Apache "Apache") have a type of [fiddle](/wiki/Apache_fiddle "Apache fiddle"), and [fiddles](/wiki/Fiddle "Fiddle") are also found many [First Nations](/wiki/First_Nations_in_Canada "First Nations in Canada") and [Métis](/wiki/M%C3%A9tis "Métis") cultures.

The music of the Indigenous peoples of Central Mexico and Central America, like that of the North American cultures, tends to be spiritual ceremonies. It traditionally includes a large variety of percussion and [wind instruments](/wiki/Wind_instrument "Wind instrument") such as drums, flutes, sea shells (used as trumpets), and "rain" tubes. No remnants of pre-Columbian stringed instruments were found until archaeologists discovered a jar in Guatemala, attributed to the Maya of the Late Classic Era (600–900 CE); this jar was decorated with imagery depicting a stringed musical instrument, which has since been reproduced. This instrument is one of the very few stringed instruments known in the Americas before the introduction of European [musical instruments](/wiki/Musical_instrument "Musical instrument"); when played, it produces a sound that mimics a jaguar's growl.[[195]](#cite_note-196)

[Visual arts by Indigenous peoples of the Americas](/wiki/Visual_arts_by_Indigenous_peoples_of_the_Americas "Visual arts by Indigenous peoples of the Americas") comprise a major category in the world [art collection](/wiki/Collection_(museum) "Collection (museum)"). Contributions include [pottery](/wiki/Native_American_pottery "Native American pottery"), [paintings](/wiki/Painting "Painting"), [jewelry](/wiki/Jewellery "Jewellery"), [weavings](/wiki/Weaving "Weaving"), [sculptures](/wiki/Sculpture "Sculpture"), [basketry](/wiki/Basketry "Basketry"), [carvings](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/carving "wikt:carving"), and [beadwork](/wiki/Beadwork "Beadwork").[[196]](#cite_note-197) Because too many artists were posing as Native Americans and Alaska Natives[[197]](#cite_note-198) to profit from the cachet of Indigenous art in the United States, the U.S. passed the [Indian Arts and Crafts Act of 1990](/wiki/Indian_Arts_and_Crafts_Act_of_1990 "Indian Arts and Crafts Act of 1990"), requiring artists to prove that they were enrolled in a [state](/wiki/State_recognized_tribes "State recognized tribes") or [federally recognized tribe](/wiki/Federally_recognized_tribe "Federally recognized tribe"). To support the ongoing practice of [American Indian](/wiki/Native_Americans_in_the_United_States "Native Americans in the United States"), [Alaska Native](/wiki/Alaska_Natives "Alaska Natives"), and [Native Hawaiian](/wiki/Native_Hawaiians "Native Hawaiians") arts and cultures in the United States,[[198]](#cite_note-199) the Ford Foundation, arts advocates, and American Indian tribes created an endowment seed fund and established a national Native Arts and Cultures Foundation in 2007.[[199]](#cite_note-200)[[200]](#cite_note-201)

After the entry of the Spaniards, the process of spiritual conquest was favored, among other things, by the [liturgical](/wiki/Liturgical "Liturgical") musical service to which the natives, whose musical gifts came to surprise the missionaries, were integrated. The musical gifts of the natives were of such magnitude that they soon learned the rules of counterpoint and [polyphony](/wiki/Polyphony "Polyphony") and even the virtuous handling of the instruments. This helped to ensure that it was not necessary to bring more musicians from Spain, which significantly annoyed the clergy.[[201]](#cite_note-202)

The solution that was proposed was not to employ but a certain number of Indigenous people in the musical service, not to teach them counterpoint, not to allow them to play certain instruments ([brass](/wiki/Brass_instrument "Brass instrument") breaths, for example, in [Oaxaca](/wiki/Oaxaca "Oaxaca"), Mexico) and, finally, not to import more instruments so that the Indigenous people would not have access to them. The latter was not an obstacle to the musical enjoyment of the natives, who experienced the making of instruments, particularly rubbed strings ([violins](/wiki/Violin "Violin") and [double basses](/wiki/Double_bass "Double bass")) or plucked (third). It is there where we can find the origin of what is now called traditional music whose instruments have their tuning and a typical Western structure.[[202]](#cite_note-203)

## History and status by continent and country

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Indigenous_peoples_of_the_Americas&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: History and status by continent and country")]

### North America

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Indigenous_peoples_of_the_Americas&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: North America")]

#### Canada

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Indigenous_peoples_of_the_Americas&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Canada")]

Main article: [Indigenous peoples in Canada](/wiki/Indigenous_peoples_in_Canada "Indigenous peoples in Canada")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/db/Indigenous_population_by_census_division.svg/330px-Indigenous_population_by_census_division.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Indigenous_population_by_census_division.svg)

A map of Canada showing the percent of [self-reported Indigenous identity](/wiki/Population_of_Canada#Indigenous_peoples "Population of Canada") (First Nations, Inuit, Métis) by census division, according to the [2021 Canadian census](/wiki/2021_Canadian_census "2021 Canadian census")[[203]](#cite_note-Statistics_Canada_2022_u867-204)

These paragraphs are an excerpt from [Indigenous peoples in Canada](/wiki/Indigenous_peoples_in_Canada "Indigenous peoples in Canada").[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Indigenous_peoples_in_Canada&action=edit)]
