Aboriginal Canadians are the Indigenous peoples within the boundaries of [Canada](/wiki/Canada "Canada").[[204]](#cite_note-Indigenous_peoples_in_Canada_indian-205) They comprise the [First Nations](/wiki/First_Nations_in_Canada "First Nations in Canada"),[[205]](#cite_note-Indigenous_peoples_in_Canada_First_Nations_Culture_Areas_Index-206) [Inuit](/wiki/Inuit "Inuit"),[[206]](#cite_note-Indigenous_peoples_in_Canada_ICCcharter-207) and [Métis](/wiki/M%C3%A9tis#Métis_people_in_Canada "Métis"),[[207]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEToddThorntonCollins200110-208) representing roughly 5.0% of the total [Canadian population](/wiki/Population_of_Canada "Population of Canada"). There are over 600 recognized [First Nations governments](/wiki/List_of_First_Nations_peoples_in_Canada "List of First Nations peoples in Canada") or [bands](/wiki/Band_government "Band government") with distinctive cultures, languages, music and other arts.[[208]](#cite_note-Indigenous_peoples_in_Canada_Aboriginal_Identity_2011-209)[[209]](#cite_note-Indigenous_peoples_in_Canada_three2-210)

[Old Crow Flats](/wiki/Old_Crow_Flats "Old Crow Flats") and [Bluefish Caves](/wiki/Bluefish_Caves "Bluefish Caves") are some of the earliest known sites of human habitation in Canada.[[210]](#cite_note-211) The characteristics of Indigenous cultures in Canada prior to European colonization included permanent settlements,[[211]](#cite_note-212) agriculture,[[212]](#cite_note-213) civic and ceremonial architecture,[[213]](#cite_note-214) complex [societal hierarchies](/wiki/Hierarchy "Hierarchy"), and [trading networks](/wiki/Trade "Trade").[[214]](#cite_note-215) Métis nations of mixed ancestry originated in the mid-17th century when First Nations and Inuit married Europeans, primarily [French settlers](/wiki/French_colonization_of_the_Americas "French colonization of the Americas").[[215]](#cite_note-Indigenous_peoples_in_Canada_testt-216) First Nations and Métis peoples played a critical part in the development of European colonies in Canada, particularly for their role in assisting Europeans during the [North American fur trade](/wiki/North_American_fur_trade "North American fur trade").

Various [Aboriginal laws](/wiki/Canadian_Aboriginal_law "Canadian Aboriginal law"), [treaties](/wiki/Treaty#Canada "Treaty"), and legislation have been enacted between European immigrants and Indigenous groups across Canada. The impact of [settler colonialism in Canada](/wiki/Settler_colonialism_in_Canada "Settler colonialism in Canada") can be seen in its culture, history, politics, laws, and legislatures.[[216]](#cite_note-Indigenous_peoples_in_Canada_y517-217) Historically, this included assimilationist policies affecting Indigenous languages, traditions, religion and the degradation of Indigenous communities that has contemporarily been described by some, including academics and politicians, as a [cultural genocide, or genocide](/wiki/Canadian_genocide_of_Indigenous_peoples "Canadian genocide of Indigenous peoples").[[217]](#cite_note-218)

The modern Indigenous right to [self-government](/wiki/Self-governance "Self-governance") provides for [Indigenous self-government in Canada](/wiki/Indigenous_self-government_in_Canada "Indigenous self-government in Canada") and the management of cultural, political, health and economic responsibilities within Indigenous communities. [National Indigenous Peoples Day](/wiki/National_Indigenous_Peoples_Day "National Indigenous Peoples Day") recognizes the vast cultures and contributions of Indigenous peoples to the [history of Canada](/wiki/History_of_Canada "History of Canada").[[218]](#cite_note-Indigenous_peoples_in_Canada_history-219) First Nations, Inuit, and Métis peoples of all backgrounds have become [prominent figures](/wiki/Indigenous_Canadian_personalities "Indigenous Canadian personalities") in Canada and have helped shape the [Canadian cultural identity](/wiki/Culture_of_Canada "Culture of Canada").[[219]](#cite_note-220)

#### Greenland

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Indigenous_peoples_of_the_Americas&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Greenland")]

Main article: [Greenlandic Inuit](/wiki/Greenlandic_Inuit "Greenlandic Inuit")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b4/Kulusuk%2C_Inuit_couple_%286822265499%29.jpg/250px-Kulusuk%2C_Inuit_couple_%286822265499%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Kulusuk,_Inuit_couple_(6822265499).jpg)

[Tunumiit](/wiki/Tunumiit "Tunumiit") [Inuit](/wiki/Inuit "Inuit") couple from [Kulusuk](/wiki/Kulusuk "Kulusuk"), Greenland

The Greenlandic Inuit ([Kalaallisut](/wiki/West_Greenlandic "West Greenlandic"): *kalaallit*, [Tunumiisut](/wiki/Tunumiisut "Tunumiisut"): *tunumiit*, [Inuktun](/wiki/Inuktun "Inuktun"): *inughuit*) are the [Indigenous](/wiki/Indigenous_peoples "Indigenous peoples") and most populous ethnic group in [Greenland](/wiki/Greenland "Greenland").[[220]](#cite_note-221) This means that [Denmark](/wiki/Denmark "Denmark") has one officially recognized [Indigenous group](/wiki/Indigenous_group "Indigenous group"). the [Inuit](/wiki/Inuit "Inuit") – the [Greenlandic Inuit](/wiki/Greenlandic_Inuit "Greenlandic Inuit") of Greenland and the [Greenlandic people in Denmark](/wiki/Greenlandic_people_in_Denmark "Greenlandic people in Denmark") (Inuit residing in Denmark).

Approximately 89 percent of Greenland's population of 57,695 is Greenlandic [Inuit](/wiki/Inuit "Inuit"), or 51,349 people as of 2012[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Indigenous_peoples_of_the_Americas&action=edit).[[37]](#cite_note-cia-38)[[221]](#cite_note-ethno-222) [Ethnographically](/wiki/Ethnographically "Ethnographically"), they consist of three major groups:

* the [Kalaallit](/wiki/Kalaallit "Kalaallit") of west Greenland, who speak [Kalaallisut](/wiki/West_Greenlandic "West Greenlandic")
* the [Tunumiit](/wiki/Tunumiit "Tunumiit") of [Tunu](/wiki/Tunu "Tunu") (east Greenland), who speak [Tunumiit oraasiat](/wiki/Tunumiit_language "Tunumiit language") ("East Greenlandic")
* the [Inughuit](/wiki/Inughuit "Inughuit") of north Greenland, who speak [Inuktun](/wiki/Inuktun "Inuktun") ("Polar Inuit")

#### Mexico

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Indigenous_peoples_of_the_Americas&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Mexico")]

Main article: [Indigenous peoples of Mexico](/wiki/Indigenous_peoples_of_Mexico "Indigenous peoples of Mexico")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a7/Distribution_of_indigenous_people_in_Mexico%2C_2020.svg/250px-Distribution_of_indigenous_people_in_Mexico%2C_2020.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Distribution_of_indigenous_people_in_Mexico,_2020.svg)

Proportion of [Native Mexicans](/wiki/Indigenous_peoples_of_Mexico "Indigenous peoples of Mexico") in each municipalities in 2020.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/84/Huichol_indian.jpg/250px-Huichol_indian.jpg)](/wiki/File:Huichol_indian.jpg)

A [Huichol](/wiki/Huichol_people "Huichol people") woman from [Zacatecas](/wiki/Zacatecas "Zacatecas"), Mexico

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/92/Carnavaltenejapa.JPG/250px-Carnavaltenejapa.JPG)](/wiki/File:Carnavaltenejapa.JPG)

A carnival with [Tzeltal people](/wiki/Tzeltal_people "Tzeltal people") in [Tenejapa Municipality](/wiki/Tenejapa_Municipality "Tenejapa Municipality"), Chiapas

The territory of modern-day Mexico was home to numerous Indigenous civilizations before the arrival of the Spanish *[conquistadores](/wiki/Conquistador "Conquistador")*: The [Olmecs](/wiki/Olmec "Olmec"), who flourished from between 1200 BCE to about 400 BCE in the coastal regions of the [Gulf of Mexico](/wiki/Gulf_of_Mexico "Gulf of Mexico"); the [Zapotecs](/wiki/Zapotec_peoples "Zapotec peoples") and the [Mixtecs](/wiki/Mixtec "Mixtec"), who held sway in the mountains of [Oaxaca](/wiki/Oaxaca "Oaxaca") and the [Isthmus of Tehuantepec](/wiki/Isthmus_of_Tehuantepec "Isthmus of Tehuantepec"); the [Maya](/wiki/Maya_civilization "Maya civilization") in the [Yucatán](/wiki/Yucat%C3%A1n "Yucatán") (and into neighboring areas of contemporary Central America); the [Purépecha](/wiki/Pur%C3%A9pecha_people "Purépecha people") in present-day [Michoacán](/wiki/Michoac%C3%A1n "Michoacán") and surrounding areas, and the [Aztecs](/wiki/Aztec "Aztec")/[Mexica](/wiki/Mexica "Mexica"), who, from their central capital at [Tenochtitlan](/wiki/Tenochtitlan "Tenochtitlan"), dominated much of the center and south of the country (and the non-Aztec inhabitants of those areas) when [Hernán Cortés](/wiki/Hern%C3%A1n_Cort%C3%A9s "Hernán Cortés") first landed at [Veracruz](/wiki/Veracruz_(city) "Veracruz (city)").

In contrast to what was the general rule in the rest of North America, the history of the colony of [New Spain](/wiki/New_Spain "New Spain") was one of racial intermingling (*mestizaje*). *[Mestizos](/wiki/Mestizo "Mestizo")*, which in Mexico designate people who do not identify culturally with any Indigenous grouping, quickly came to account for a majority of the colony's population. Today, [Mestizos in Mexico](/wiki/Mestizos_in_Mexico "Mestizos in Mexico") of mixed Indigenous and European ancestry (with a minor African contribution) are still a majority of the population. Genetic studies vary over whether Indigenous or European ancestry predominates in the Mexican Mestizo population.[[222]](#cite_note-223)[[223]](#cite_note-224) In the 2020 [INEGI](/wiki/National_Institute_of_Statistics_and_Geography "National Institute of Statistics and Geography") census, 23.2 million people (19.4% of the Mexican population aged 3 years and older) self-identified as Indigenous.[[2]](#cite_note-INEGI2020_SELF-IDENTIFICATION-2) Somewhat contradictorily, in the same 2020 census, 11.8 million people (9.3% of the Mexican population) were determined to be Indigenous by the Mexican government based on the language spoken in their households.[[1]](#cite_note-INEGI2020_LANGUAGE-1) The Indigenous population is distributed throughout the territory of Mexico but is especially concentrated in the [Sierra Madre del Sur](/wiki/Sierra_Madre_del_Sur "Sierra Madre del Sur"), the [Yucatán Peninsula](/wiki/Yucat%C3%A1n_Peninsula "Yucatán Peninsula"), and the most remote and difficult-to-access areas, such as the [Sierra Madre Oriental](/wiki/Sierra_Madre_Oriental "Sierra Madre Oriental"), the [Sierra Madre Occidental](/wiki/Sierra_Madre_Occidental "Sierra Madre Occidental"), and neighboring areas.[[224]](#cite_note-225) The [CDI](/wiki/National_Commission_for_the_Development_of_Indigenous_Peoples "National Commission for the Development of Indigenous Peoples") identifies 62 Indigenous groups in Mexico, each with a unique language.[[225]](#cite_note-226)[[226]](#cite_note-227)

In the states of [Chiapas](/wiki/Chiapas "Chiapas") and [Oaxaca](/wiki/Oaxaca "Oaxaca") and the interior of the [Yucatán Peninsula](/wiki/Yucat%C3%A1n_Peninsula "Yucatán Peninsula"), a large amount of the population is of Indigenous descent with the largest ethnic group being Maya with a population of 900,000.[[227]](#cite_note-228) Large Indigenous minorities, including [Aztecs](/wiki/Aztecs "Aztecs") or [Nahua](/wiki/Nahuas "Nahuas"), [Purépechas](/wiki/Pur%C3%A9pecha_people "Purépecha people"), [Mazahua](/wiki/Mazahua_people "Mazahua people"), [Otomi](/wiki/Otomi_people "Otomi people"), and [Mixtecs](/wiki/Mixtec "Mixtec") are also present in the central regions of Mexico. In the [Northern](/wiki/Northern_Mexico "Northern Mexico") and [Bajio](/wiki/Baj%C3%ADo "Bajío") regions of Mexico, Indigenous people are a small minority.

The [General Law of Linguistic Rights of the Indigenous Peoples](/wiki/Ley_General_de_Derechos_Ling%C3%BC%C3%ADsticos_de_los_Pueblos_Ind%C3%ADgenas "Ley General de Derechos Lingüísticos de los Pueblos Indígenas") grants all Indigenous languages spoken in Mexico, regardless of the number of speakers, the same validity as Spanish in all territories in which they are spoken, and Indigenous peoples are entitled to request some [public services](/wiki/Public_services "Public services") and documents in their native languages.[[228]](#cite_note-229) Along with Spanish, the law has granted them—more than 60 languages—the status of "national languages". The law includes all Indigenous languages of the Americas regardless of origin; that is, it includes Indigenous languages of ethnic groups non-native to the territory. The [National Commission for the Development of Indigenous Peoples](/wiki/National_Commission_for_the_Development_of_Indigenous_Peoples "National Commission for the Development of Indigenous Peoples") recognizes the language of the [Kickapoo](/wiki/Kickapoo_people "Kickapoo people"), who immigrated from the United States[[229]](#cite_note-230) and recognizes the languages of the Indigenous refugees from Guatemala.[[230]](#cite_note-231) The Mexican government has promoted and established bilingual primary and [secondary education](/wiki/Secondary_education "Secondary education") in some Indigenous rural communities. Nonetheless, of the Indigenous peoples in Mexico, 93% are either native speakers or bilingual second-language speakers of Spanish with only about 62.4% of them (or 5.4% of the country's population) speaking an Indigenous language and about a sixth do not speak Spanish (0.7% of the country's population).[[231]](#cite_note-232)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/64/Copper_Canyons_marathon_in_Urique.jpg/250px-Copper_Canyons_marathon_in_Urique.jpg)](/wiki/File:Copper_Canyons_marathon_in_Urique.jpg)

The [Rarámuri](/wiki/Rar%C3%A1muri "Rarámuri") marathon in [Urique](/wiki/Urique "Urique")

The Indigenous peoples in Mexico have the right of free determination under the second article of the constitution. According to this article, the Indigenous peoples are granted:[[232]](#cite_note-233)

* the right to decide the internal forms of social, economic, political, and cultural organization;
* the right to apply their normative systems of regulation as long as [human rights](/wiki/Human_rights "Human rights") and [gender equality](/wiki/Gender_equality "Gender equality") are respected;
* the right to preserve and enrich their languages and cultures;
* the right to elect representatives before the [municipal council](/wiki/Municipal_council "Municipal council") in which their territories are located;

amongst other rights.

#### United States

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Indigenous_peoples_of_the_Americas&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: United States")]

Main articles: [Native Americans in the United States](/wiki/Native_Americans_in_the_United_States "Native Americans in the United States") and [Alaska Natives](/wiki/Alaska_Natives "Alaska Natives")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7b/Indigenous_Americans_by_county.png/250px-Indigenous_Americans_by_county.png)](/wiki/File:Indigenous_Americans_by_county.png)

Proportion of [Native Americans](/wiki/Native_Americans_in_the_United_States "Native Americans in the United States") in each county in 2020.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/97/Norma_howard_choctaw.jpg/250px-Norma_howard_choctaw.jpg)](/wiki/File:Norma_howard_choctaw.jpg)

A [Choctaw](/wiki/Choctaw "Choctaw") artist in present-day [Oklahoma](/wiki/Oklahoma "Oklahoma")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8d/Navajo_Cowboy-1.jpg/250px-Navajo_Cowboy-1.jpg)](/wiki/File:Navajo_Cowboy-1.jpg)

A [Navajo](/wiki/Navajo "Navajo") man on horseback in present-day [Monument Valley](/wiki/Monument_Valley "Monument Valley") in [Arizona](/wiki/Arizona "Arizona")

Indigenous peoples in what is now the [contiguous United States](/wiki/Contiguous_United_States "Contiguous United States"), including their descendants, were commonly called American Indians, or simply Indians domestically and since the late 20th century the term Native American came into [common use](/wiki/Native_American_name_controversy#"Native_American"_(since_the_1960s) "Native American name controversy"). In Alaska, Indigenous peoples belong to 11 cultures with 11 languages. These include the [St. Lawrence Island Yupik](/wiki/St._Lawrence_Island_Yupik "St. Lawrence Island Yupik"), [Iñupiat](/wiki/I%C3%B1upiat "Iñupiat"), [Athabaskan](/wiki/Alaskan_Athabaskans "Alaskan Athabaskans"), [Yup'ik](/wiki/Yup%27ik "Yup'ik"), [Cup'ik](/wiki/Cup%27ik "Cup'ik"), [Unangax](/wiki/Unangax "Unangax"), [Alutiiq](/wiki/Alutiiq "Alutiiq"), [Eyak](/wiki/Eyak "Eyak"), [Haida](/wiki/Haida_people "Haida people"), [Tsimshian](/wiki/Tsimshian "Tsimshian"), and [Tlingit](/wiki/Tlingit "Tlingit"),[[233]](#cite_note-234) and are collectively called [Alaska Natives](/wiki/Alaska_Natives "Alaska Natives"). They include Native American peoples as well as Inuit, who are distinct but occupy areas of the region.

The United States has authority over Indigenous [Polynesian people](/wiki/Polynesia "Polynesia"), which include [Hawaiians](/wiki/Native_Hawaiians "Native Hawaiians"), [Marshallese](/wiki/Marshallese_people "Marshallese people") (Micronesian), and [Samoan](/wiki/Samoan_people "Samoan people"); politically they are classified as [Pacific Islander Americans](/wiki/Pacific_Islander_Americans "Pacific Islander Americans"). They are geographically, genetically, and culturally distinct from Indigenous peoples of the mainland continents of the Americas.

In the 2020 census 2.9% of the U.S. population claimed to have some degree of Native American heritage. When answering a question about racial background, 3.7 million people identified solely as "American Indian or Alaska Native", while another 5.9 million did so in combination with other races.[[234]](#cite_note-USCENSUS2020-235) [Aztecs](/wiki/Aztecs "Aztecs") were the largest single Native American group in the 2020 census, while [Cherokee](/wiki/Cherokee "Cherokee") was the largest group in combination with any other race.[[235]](#cite_note-236) Tribes have established their criteria for membership, which are often based on [blood quantum](/wiki/Blood_quantum "Blood quantum"), [lineal descent](/wiki/Lineal_descent "Lineal descent"), or residency. A minority of Native Americans live in land units called [Indian reservations](/wiki/Indian_reservation "Indian reservation").

Some California and Southwestern tribes, such as the [Kumeyaay](/wiki/Kumeyaay "Kumeyaay"), [Cocopa](/wiki/Cocopa "Cocopa"), [Pascua Yaqui](/wiki/Pascua_Yaqui "Pascua Yaqui"), [Tohono O'odham](/wiki/Tohono_O%27odham "Tohono O'odham"), and [Apache](/wiki/Apache "Apache"), span both sides of the US–Mexican border. By treaty, [Haudenosaunee people](/wiki/Iroquois "Iroquois") have the legal right to freely cross the US–Canada border. [Athabascan](/wiki/Athabaskan_languages "Athabaskan languages"), [Tlingit](/wiki/Tlingit "Tlingit"), [Haida](/wiki/Haida_people "Haida people"), [Tsimshian](/wiki/Tsimshian_people "Tsimshian people"), [Iñupiat](/wiki/Inupiat_people "Inupiat people"), [Blackfeet](/wiki/Blackfoot_Confederacy "Blackfoot Confederacy"), [Nakota](/wiki/Nakota "Nakota"), [Cree](/wiki/Cree "Cree"), [Anishinaabe](/wiki/Anishinaabe "Anishinaabe"), [Huron](/wiki/Wyandot_people "Wyandot people"), [Lenape](/wiki/Lenape "Lenape"), [Mi'kmaq](/wiki/Mi%27kmaq_people "Mi'kmaq people"), [Penobscot](/wiki/Penobscot_people "Penobscot people"), and Haudenosaunee, among others, live in both Canada and the United States, whose international border intersects their common cultural territory.

### Central America

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Indigenous_peoples_of_the_Americas&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Central America")]

#### Belize

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Indigenous_peoples_of_the_Americas&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Belize")]

Mestizos (mixed European-Indigenous) number about 34% of the population; unmixed [Maya](/wiki/Maya_peoples "Maya peoples") make up another 10.6% ([Kekchi](/wiki/Q%27eqchi%27_people "Q'eqchi' people"), [Mopan](/wiki/Mopan_people "Mopan people"), and [Yucatec](/wiki/Yucatec "Yucatec")). The [Garifuna](/wiki/Garifuna_people "Garifuna people"), who came to Belize in the 19th century from [Saint Vincent and the Grenadines](/wiki/Saint_Vincent_and_the_Grenadines "Saint Vincent and the Grenadines"), have [mixed African](/wiki/Zambo "Zambo"), [Carib](/wiki/Island_Caribs "Island Caribs"), and [Arawak](/wiki/Arawak "Arawak") ancestry and make up another 6% of the population.[[236]](#cite_note-celade-237)

#### Costa Rica

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Indigenous_peoples_of_the_Americas&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Costa Rica")]

Main article: [Indigenous peoples of Costa Rica](/wiki/Indigenous_peoples_of_Costa_Rica "Indigenous peoples of Costa Rica")

There are over 114,000 inhabitants of Native American origins, representing 2.4% of the population. Most of them live in secluded reservations, distributed among eight ethnic groups: [Quitirrisí](/wiki/Quitirris%C3%AD "Quitirrisí") (In the Central Valley), [Matambú](/wiki/Matamb%C3%BA "Matambú") or [Chorotega](/wiki/Chorotega_people "Chorotega people") (Guanacaste), [Maleku](/wiki/Maleku_people "Maleku people") (Northern Alajuela), [Bribri](/wiki/Bribri_people "Bribri people") (Southern Atlantic), [Cabécar](/wiki/Cab%C3%A9car_people "Cabécar people") (Cordillera de Talamanca), [Boruca](/wiki/Boruca_people "Boruca people") (Southern Costa Rica) and [Ngäbe](/wiki/Ng%C3%A4be_people "Ngäbe people") (Southern Costa Rica along the Panamá border).

These native groups are characterized by their work in wood, like masks, drums, and other artistic figures, as well as fabrics made of cotton.

#### El Salvador

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Indigenous_peoples_of_the_Americas&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: El Salvador")]

Main articles: [Demographics of El Salvador](/wiki/Demographics_of_El_Salvador "Demographics of El Salvador") and [La Matanza](/wiki/La_Matanza "La Matanza")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/43/Nuestros_Angeles_de_El_Salvador.jpg/250px-Nuestros_Angeles_de_El_Salvador.jpg)](/wiki/File:Nuestros_Angeles_de_El_Salvador.jpg)

Indigenous [Pipil](/wiki/Pipil_people "Pipil people") women dancing in the traditional Procession of Palms in [Panchimalco](/wiki/Panchimalco "Panchimalco"), El Salvador

Estimates for El Salvador's Indigenous population vary. The 2024 census found that 1.2% of the population, or 68,148 people identified as Indigenous.[[22]](#cite_note-infodemia-23) Historically, estimates have claimed higher amounts. A 1930 census stated that 5.6% were Indigenous.[[237]](#cite_note-1930_El_Salvador-238) By the mid-20th century, there may have been as much as 20% (or 400,000) that would qualify as "Indigenous". Another estimate stated that by the late 1980s, 10% of the population was Indigenous, and another 89% was mestizo (or people of mixed European and Indigenous ancestry).[[238]](#cite_note-ELS_Study-239)

Much of [El Salvador](/wiki/El_Salvador "El Salvador") was home to various Indigenous groups like the [Pipil](/wiki/Pipil_people "Pipil people"), the [Lenca](/wiki/Lenca_people "Lenca people"), [Mayas](/wiki/Maya_peoples "Maya peoples") ([Chorti](/wiki/Ch%CA%BCorti%CA%BC_people "Chʼortiʼ people") and [Poqomam](/wiki/Poqomam_people "Poqomam people")), [Chorotegas](/wiki/Mangue_language "Mangue language"), and [Xincas](/wiki/Xinca_people "Xinca people"). The Pipil lived in western [El Salvador](/wiki/El_Salvador "El Salvador"), spoke [Nawat](/wiki/Nahuat "Nahuat"), and had many settlements there, most notably [Cuzcatlan](/wiki/Cuzcatlan "Cuzcatlan"). The Pipil had no precious mineral resources, but they did have rich and [fertile land](/wiki/Fertile_soil "Fertile soil") that was good for farming. The Spaniards were disappointed not to find gold or jewels in [El Salvador](/wiki/El_Salvador "El Salvador") as they had in other lands like [Guatemala](/wiki/Guatemala "Guatemala") or Mexico, but upon learning of the fertile land in El Salvador, they attempted to conquer it. Noted Mesoamerican Indigenous warriors who rose against the Spanish included Princess Atonal and Atlacatl of the Pipil people in central El Salvador, and Princess Antu Silan Ulap of the Lenca people in eastern El Salvador, who saw the Spanish not as gods but as barbaric invaders. After fierce battles, the Pipil successfully fought off the Spanish army led by [Pedro de Alvarado](/wiki/Pedro_de_Alvarado "Pedro de Alvarado") along with their Indigenous allies (the Tlaxcalas), sending them back to Guatemala. After many other attacks with an army reinforced with Indigenous allies, the Spanish were able to conquer Cuzcatlan. After further attacks, the Spanish also conquered the Lenca people. Eventually, the Spaniards intermarried with Pipil and Lenca women, resulting in the mestizo population that would make up the vast majority of the Salvadoran people. Today many Pipil and other Indigenous populations live in the many small towns of El Salvador like [Izalco](/wiki/Izalco "Izalco"), [Panchimalco](/wiki/Panchimalco "Panchimalco"), [Sacacoyo](/wiki/Sacacoyo "Sacacoyo"), and [Nahuizalco](/wiki/Nahuizalco "Nahuizalco").

#### Guatemala

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Indigenous_peoples_of_the_Americas&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: Guatemala")]

Main article: [Indigenous peoples in Guatemala](/wiki/Indigenous_peoples_in_Guatemala "Indigenous peoples in Guatemala")

See also: [Demographics of Guatemala](/wiki/Demographics_of_Guatemala "Demographics of Guatemala")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8e/Guat%C3%A9mala_78.jpg/250px-Guat%C3%A9mala_78.jpg)](/wiki/File:Guat%C3%A9mala_78.jpg)

Modern Maya people in [Sololá](/wiki/Solol%C3%A1_Department "Sololá Department"), Guatemala

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6f/Image_of_photo_of_Mayan_woman_%286849889936%29.jpg/250px-Image_of_photo_of_Mayan_woman_%286849889936%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Image_of_photo_of_Mayan_woman_(6849889936).jpg)

An elderly Maya woman

[Guatemala](/wiki/Guatemala "Guatemala") has one of the largest Indigenous populations in [Central America](/wiki/Central_America "Central America"), with approximately 43.6% of the population considering themselves Indigenous.[[239]](#cite_note-cia.gov-240) The [Indigenous](/wiki/Indigenous_peoples "Indigenous peoples") demographic portion of Guatemala's population consists of a majority of Maya groups and one non-Maya group. The Mayan language-speaking portion makes up 29.7% of the population and is distributed into 23 groups namely [Q'eqchi'](/wiki/Q%CA%BCeqchi%CA%BC "Qʼeqchiʼ") 8.3%, [K'iche](/wiki/K%CA%BCiche%CA%BC_people "Kʼicheʼ people") 7.8%, [Mam](/wiki/Mam_people "Mam people") 4.4%, [Kaqchikel](/wiki/Kaqchikel_people "Kaqchikel people") 3%, Q'anjob'al 1.2%, Poqomchi' 1%, and Other 4%.[[239]](#cite_note-cia.gov-240) The non-Maya group consists of the [Xinca](/wiki/Xinca_people "Xinca people") who are another set of Indigenous people making up 1.8% of the population.[[239]](#cite_note-cia.gov-240) Other sources indicate that between 50% and 60% of the population could be Indigenous because part of the Mestizo population is predominantly Indigenous.

The Maya peoples cover a vast geographic area throughout Central America and expand beyond Guatemala into other countries. One could find vast groups of Maya people in Boca Costa, in the Southern portions of Guatemala, as well as the Western Highlands living together in close communities.[[240]](#cite_note-241) Within these communities and outside of them, around 23 [Indigenous languages](/wiki/Indigenous_language "Indigenous language") (or [Native American Indigenous languages](/wiki/Indigenous_languages_of_the_Americas "Indigenous languages of the Americas")) are spoken as a first language. Of these 23 languages, they only received official recognition by the Government in 2003 under the Law of National Languages.[[239]](#cite_note-cia.gov-240) The Law on National Languages recognizes 23 Indigenous languages including Xinca, enforcing that public and government institutions not only translate but also provide services in said languages.[[241]](#cite_note-242) It would provide services in [Cakchiquel](/wiki/Kaqchikel_language "Kaqchikel language"), [Garifuna](/wiki/Garifuna_language "Garifuna language"), [Kekchi](/wiki/Q%CA%BCeqchi%CA%BC_language "Qʼeqchiʼ language"), [Mam](/wiki/Mam_language "Mam language"), [Quiche](/wiki/K%CA%BCiche%CA%BC_language "Kʼicheʼ language"), and [Xinca](/wiki/Xincan_languages "Xincan languages").[[242]](#cite_note-243)

The Law of National Languages has been an effort to grant and protect Indigenous peoples' rights not afforded to them previously. Along with the Law of National Languages passed in 2003, in 1996 the Guatemalan Constitutional Court had ratified the ILO Convention 169 on Indigenous and Tribal Peoples.[[243]](#cite_note-244) The [ILO Convention 169 on Indigenous and Tribal Peoples](/wiki/International_Labour_Organization "International Labour Organization"), is also known as Convention 169. Which is the only International Law regarding Indigenous peoples that Independent countries can adopt. The convention establishes that governments like Guatemala must consult with Indigenous groups before any projects occur on tribal lands.[[244]](#cite_note-245)

#### Honduras

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Indigenous_peoples_of_the_Americas&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: Honduras")]

Main article: [Indigenous peoples of Honduras](/wiki/Indigenous_peoples_of_Honduras "Indigenous peoples of Honduras")

About 5 percent of the population is of full-blooded Indigenous descent, but as much as 80 percent of Hondurans are [mestizo](/wiki/Mestizo "Mestizo") or part-Indigenous with European admixture, and about 10 percent are of Indigenous or [African](/wiki/Afro-Hondurans "Afro-Hondurans") descent.[[245]](#cite_note-Miskitu-246) The largest concentrations of [Indigenous communities in Honduras](/wiki/Indigenous_peoples_of_Honduras "Indigenous peoples of Honduras") are in the westernmost areas facing Guatemala and along the coast of the [Caribbean Sea](/wiki/Caribbean_Sea "Caribbean Sea"), as well as on the border with Nicaragua.[[245]](#cite_note-Miskitu-246) The majority of Indigenous people are [Lencas](/wiki/Lenca_people "Lenca people"), [Miskitos](/wiki/Miskito_people "Miskito people") to the east, [Mayas](/wiki/Maya_peoples "Maya peoples"), [Pech](/wiki/Pech_people "Pech people"), [Sumos](/wiki/Sumo_people "Sumo people"), and [Tolupan](/wiki/Tolupan_people "Tolupan people").[[245]](#cite_note-Miskitu-246)

#### Nicaragua

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Indigenous_peoples_of_the_Americas&action=edit&section=27 "Edit section: Nicaragua")]

See also: [Nicaraguan Indigenous Organizations](/wiki/Nicaraguan_Indigenous_Organizations "Nicaraguan Indigenous Organizations")

About 5 percent of the [Nicaraguan](/wiki/Nicaraguan "Nicaraguan") population is Indigenous. The largest Indigenous group in Nicaragua is the [Miskito people](/wiki/Miskito_people "Miskito people"). Their territory extended from [Cabo Camarón](/wiki/Cabo_Camar%C3%B3n "Cabo Camarón"), Honduras, to [La Cruz de Rio Grande](/wiki/La_Cruz_de_R%C3%ADo_Grande "La Cruz de Río Grande"), Nicaragua along the [Mosquito Coast](/wiki/Mosquito_Coast "Mosquito Coast"). There is a native [Miskito language](/wiki/Miskito_language "Miskito language"), but large numbers speak [Miskito Coast Creole](/wiki/Miskito_Coast_Creole "Miskito Coast Creole"), Spanish, [Rama](/wiki/Rama_language "Rama language"), and other languages. Their use of [Creole English](/wiki/English-based_creole_languages "English-based creole languages") came about through frequent contact with the British, who colonized the area. Many Miskitos are Christians. Traditional Miskito society was highly structured, politically and otherwise. It had a king, but he did not have total power. Instead, the power was split between himself, a [Miskito Governor](/wiki/Miskito_Governor "Miskito Governor"), a [Miskito General](/wiki/Miskito_General "Miskito General"), and by the 1750s, a [Miskito Admiral](/wiki/Miskito_Admiral "Miskito Admiral"). Historical information on Miskito kings is often obscured by the fact that many of the kings were semi-[mythical](/wiki/Mythology "Mythology").

Another major Indigenous culture in eastern Nicaragua is the [Mayangna (or Sumu) people](/wiki/Sumo_people "Sumo people"), counting some 10,000 people.[[246]](#cite_note-247) A smaller Indigenous culture in southeastern Nicaragua is the [Rama](/wiki/Rama_people "Rama people").

Other Indigenous groups in Nicaragua are located in the central, northern, and Pacific areas and they are self-identified as follows: [Chorotega](/wiki/Chorotega_people "Chorotega people"), [Cacaopera (or Matagalpa)](/wiki/Cacaopera_people "Cacaopera people"), [Xiu-Subtiaba](/wiki/Subtiaba_language "Subtiaba language"), and [Nicarao](/wiki/Nicarao_people "Nicarao people").[[247]](#cite_note-248)

#### Panama

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Indigenous_peoples_of_the_Americas&action=edit&section=28 "Edit section: Panama")]

See also: [Indigenous peoples of Panama](/wiki/Indigenous_peoples_of_Panama "Indigenous peoples of Panama")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/db/Panama_Embera0604.jpg/250px-Panama_Embera0604.jpg)](/wiki/File:Panama_Embera0604.jpg)

[Embera](/wiki/Embera-Wounaan "Embera-Wounaan") girl in the [Darién Province](/wiki/Dari%C3%A9n_Province "Darién Province"), 2006.

Indigenous peoples of Panama, or Native Panamanians, are the Native peoples of [Panama](/wiki/Panama "Panama"). As of the 2023 census, Indigenous peoples constitute 17.2% of Panama's population of 4.5 million, totaling just over 698,000 individuals. The [Ngäbe](/wiki/Ng%C3%A4be "Ngäbe") and [Buglé](/wiki/Bokota_people "Bokota people") comprise half of the Indigenous peoples of Panama.[[248]](#cite_note-ip-249)

Many of the Indigenous Peoples live on *[comarca indígenas](/wiki/Comarca_ind%C3%ADgena "Comarca indígena")*,[[249]](#cite_note-iwgia-250) which are administrative regions for areas with substantial [Indigenous](/wiki/Amerindian "Amerindian") populations. Three *comarcas* ([Comarca Emberá-Wounaan](/wiki/Comarca_Ember%C3%A1-Wounaan "Comarca Emberá-Wounaan"), [Guna Yala](/wiki/Guna_Yala "Guna Yala"), [Ngäbe-Buglé](/wiki/Ng%C3%A4be-Bugl%C3%A9 "Ngäbe-Buglé")) exist as equivalent to a province, with two smaller *comarcas* (Guna de Madugandí and [Guna de Wargandí](/wiki/Guna_de_Wargand%C3%AD "Guna de Wargandí")) subordinate to a province and considered equivalent to a *[corregimiento](/wiki/Corregimiento "Corregimiento")* (municipality).

### South America

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Indigenous_peoples_of_the_Americas&action=edit&section=29 "Edit section: South America")]

Main article: [Indigenous peoples of South America](/wiki/Indigenous_peoples_of_South_America "Indigenous peoples of South America")

#### Argentina

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Indigenous_peoples_of_the_Americas&action=edit&section=30 "Edit section: Argentina")]

See also: [Indigenous peoples in Argentina](/wiki/Indigenous_peoples_in_Argentina "Indigenous peoples in Argentina") and [List of Indigenous languages in Argentina](/wiki/List_of_Indigenous_languages_in_Argentina "List of Indigenous languages in Argentina")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c3/Distribution_of_indigenous_people_in_Argentina%2C_2022.svg/250px-Distribution_of_indigenous_people_in_Argentina%2C_2022.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Distribution_of_indigenous_people_in_Argentina,_2022.svg)

Proportion of [Native Argentines](/wiki/Indigenous_peoples_in_Argentina "Indigenous peoples in Argentina") in each department in 2022.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fc/The_Tolaba_Family_-_Proprietors_of_Roadside_Cafe_en_route_to_Cachi_-_Argentina.jpg/250px-The_Tolaba_Family_-_Proprietors_of_Roadside_Cafe_en_route_to_Cachi_-_Argentina.jpg)](/wiki/File:The_Tolaba_Family_-_Proprietors_of_Roadside_Cafe_en_route_to_Cachi_-_Argentina.jpg)

Owners of a roadside cafe near [Cachi](/wiki/Cachi,_Argentina "Cachi, Argentina"), [Salta Province](/wiki/Salta_Province "Salta Province"), Argentina

In 2005, the Indigenous population living in Argentina (known as *pueblos originarios*) numbered about 600,329 (1.6% of the total population); this figure includes 457,363 people who self-identified as belonging to an Indigenous ethnic group and 142,966 who identified themselves as first-generation descendants of an Indigenous people.[[250]](#cite_note-repetida_2-251) The ten most populous Indigenous peoples are the [Mapuche](/wiki/Mapuche "Mapuche") (113,680 people), the [Kolla](/wiki/Kolla_people "Kolla people") (70,505), the [Toba](/wiki/Toba_people "Toba people") (69,452), the [Guaraní](/wiki/Guaran%C3%AD_people "Guaraní people") (68,454), the [Wichi](/wiki/Wich%C3%AD_people "Wichí people") (40,036), the [Diaguita](/wiki/Diaguita "Diaguita")–[Calchaquí](/wiki/Calchaqu%C3%AD "Calchaquí") (31,753), the [Mocoví](/wiki/Mocov%C3%AD_people "Mocoví people") (15,837), the [Huarpe](/wiki/Huarpe "Huarpe") (14,633), the [Comechingón](/wiki/Comeching%C3%B3n "Comechingón") (10,863) and the [Tehuelche](/wiki/Tehuelche_people "Tehuelche people") (10,590). Minor but important peoples are the [Quechua](/wiki/Quechua_people "Quechua people") (6,739), the [Charrúa](/wiki/Charr%C3%BAa "Charrúa") (4,511), the [Pilagá](/wiki/Pilag%C3%A1 "Pilagá") (4,465), the Chané (4,376), and the Chorote (2,613). The [Selkʼnam](/wiki/Selk%CA%BCnam_people "Selkʼnam people") (Ona) people are now virtually extinct in its pure form. The languages of the Diaguita, Tehuelche, and Selkʼnam nations have become extinct or virtually extinct: the Cacán language (spoken by Diaguitas) in the 18th century and the Selkʼnam language in the 20th century; one Tehuelche language (Southern Tehuelche) is still spoken by a handful of elderly people.

#### Bolivia

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Indigenous_peoples_of_the_Americas&action=edit&section=31 "Edit section: Bolivia")]

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
|  | This article's **[factual accuracy](/wiki/Wikipedia:Accuracy_dispute "Wikipedia:Accuracy dispute") may be compromised due to out-of-date information**. Please help update this article to reflect recent events or newly available information. *(April 2012)* |

Main article: [Indigenous peoples in Bolivia](/wiki/Indigenous_peoples_in_Bolivia "Indigenous peoples in Bolivia")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a6/Pongo_0436b.jpg/250px-Pongo_0436b.jpg)](/wiki/File:Pongo_0436b.jpg)

An Indigenous woman in traditional dress near [Cochabamba](/wiki/Cochabamba "Cochabamba"), Bolivia

In Bolivia, the 2012 National Census reported that 41% of residents over the age of 15 are of Indigenous origin. Some 3.7% report growing up with an Indigenous mother tongue but do not identify as Indigenous.[[251]](#cite_note-CEPALBol32-252) When both of these categories are totaled, and children under 15, some 66.4% of Bolivia's population was recorded as Indigenous in the 2001 Census.[[252]](#cite_note-CEPALBol42-253)

The 2021 National Census, recognizes 38 cultures, each with its language, as part of a pluri-national state. Some groups, including [CONAMAQ](/wiki/CONAMAQ "CONAMAQ") (the National Council of Ayllus and Markas of Qullasuyu), draw ethnic boundaries within the Quechua- and Aymara-speaking population, resulting in a total of 50 Indigenous peoples native to Bolivia.

The largest Indigenous ethnic groups are [Quechua](/wiki/Quechuas "Quechuas"), about 2.5 million people; [Aymara](/wiki/Aymara_people "Aymara people"), 2 million; [Chiquitano](/wiki/Chiquitano_people "Chiquitano people"), 181,000; [Guaraní](/wiki/Guarani_people "Guarani people"), 126,000; and [Mojeño](/wiki/Moje%C3%B1o "Mojeño"), 69,000. Some 124,000 belong to smaller Indigenous groups.[[253]](#cite_note-CEPALBol47-254) The [Constitution of Bolivia](/wiki/Constitution_of_Bolivia "Constitution of Bolivia"), enacted in 2009, recognizes 36 cultures, each with its language, as part of a pluri-national state. Some groups, including [CONAMAQ](/wiki/CONAMAQ "CONAMAQ") (the National Council of Ayllus and Markas of Qullasuyu), draw ethnic boundaries within the Quechua- and Aymara-speaking population, resulting in a total of 50 Indigenous peoples native to Bolivia.

Large numbers of Bolivian highland [peasants](/wiki/Peasant "Peasant") retained Indigenous language, culture, customs, and communal organization throughout the Spanish conquest and the post-independence period. They mobilized to resist various attempts at the dissolution of communal landholdings and used legal recognition of "empowered caciques" to further communal organization. Indigenous revolts took place frequently until 1953.[[254]](#cite_note-255) While the National Revolutionary Movement government began in 1952 and discouraged people identifying as Indigenous (reclassifying rural people as *campesinos*, or peasants), renewed ethnic and class militancy re-emerged in the [Katarista](/wiki/Katarismo "Katarismo") movement beginning in the 1970s.[[255]](#cite_note-256) Many lowland Indigenous peoples, mostly in the east, entered national politics through the 1990 March for Territory and Dignity organized by the [CIDOB confederation](/wiki/CIDOB_Confederation "CIDOB Confederation"). That march successfully pressured the national government to sign the [ILO Convention 169](/wiki/ILO_Convention_169 "ILO Convention 169") and to begin the still-ongoing process of recognizing and giving official titles to Indigenous territories. The 1994 Law of Popular Participation granted "grassroots territorial organizations;" these are recognized by the state and have certain rights to govern local areas.

Some radio and television programs are produced in the Quechua and Aymara languages. The constitutional reform in 1997 recognized Bolivia as a multi-lingual, pluri-ethnic society and introduced [education reform](/wiki/Education_reform "Education reform"). In 2005, for the first time in the country's history, an Indigenous Aymara, [Evo Morales](/wiki/Evo_Morales "Evo Morales"), was elected as president.

Morales began work on his "Indigenous autonomy" policy, which he launched in the eastern lowlands [department](/wiki/Departments_of_Bolivia "Departments of Bolivia") on 3 August 2009. Bolivia was the first nation in the history of South America to affirm the right of Indigenous people to self-government.[[256]](#cite_note-en.mercopress.com-257) Speaking in [Santa Cruz Department](/wiki/Santa_Cruz_Department_(Bolivia) "Santa Cruz Department (Bolivia)"), the President called it "a historic day for the peasant and Indigenous movement", saying that, though he might make errors, he would "never betray the fight started by our ancestors and the fight of the Bolivian people".[[256]](#cite_note-en.mercopress.com-257) A vote on further autonomy for jurisdictions took place in December 2009, at the same time as general elections to office. The issue divided the country.[[257]](#cite_note-Bolivian_Indians_in_historic_step-258)

At that time, Indigenous peoples voted overwhelmingly for more autonomy: five departments that had not already done so voted for it;[[258]](#cite_note-Formal-259)[[259]](#cite_note-autonomy-260) as did [Gran Chaco Province](/wiki/Gran_Chaco_Province "Gran Chaco Province") in Taríja, for regional autonomy;[[260]](#cite_note-261) and 11 of 12 municipalities that had referendums on this issue.[[258]](#cite_note-Formal-259)

#### Brazil

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Indigenous_peoples_of_the_Americas&action=edit&section=32 "Edit section: Brazil")]

See also: [Indigenous peoples in Brazil](/wiki/Indigenous_peoples_in_Brazil "Indigenous peoples in Brazil")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/23/Brazil_Native_Alone_in_2022.svg/250px-Brazil_Native_Alone_in_2022.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Brazil_Native_Alone_in_2022.svg)

Proportion of [Native Brazilians](/wiki/Indigenous_peoples_in_Brazil "Indigenous peoples in Brazil") in each department in 2022.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6a/Terena005.jpg/250px-Terena005.jpg)](/wiki/File:Terena005.jpg)

An Indigenous [Terena](/wiki/Terena_people "Terena people") man from present-day [Brazil](/wiki/Brazil "Brazil")

[Indigenous peoples of Brazil](/wiki/Indigenous_peoples_of_Brazil "Indigenous peoples of Brazil") make up 0.4% of Brazil's population, or about 817,000 people, but millions of Brazilians are mestizo or have some Indigenous ancestry.[[261]](#cite_note-Discovery-262) Indigenous peoples are found in the entire territory of Brazil, although in the 21st century, the majority of them live in [Indigenous territories](/wiki/Indigenous_territory_(Brazil) "Indigenous territory (Brazil)") in the North and Center-Western parts of the country. On 18 January 2007, [Fundação Nacional do Índio](/wiki/Funda%C3%A7%C3%A3o_Nacional_do_%C3%8Dndio "Fundação Nacional do Índio") (FUNAI) reported that it had confirmed the presence of 67 different [uncontacted tribes](/wiki/Uncontacted_peoples "Uncontacted peoples") in Brazil, up from 40 in 2005. Brazil is now the nation that has the largest number of uncontacted tribes, and the island of [New Guinea](/wiki/New_Guinea "New Guinea") is second.[[261]](#cite_note-Discovery-262)

*The Washington Post* reported in 2007, "As has been proved in the past when uncontacted tribes are introduced to other populations and the microbes they carry, maladies as simple as the common cold can be deadly. In the 1970s, 185 members of the [Panara](/wiki/Panar%C3%A1_people "Panará people") tribe died within two years of discovery after contracting such diseases as flu and chickenpox, leaving only 69 survivors."[[262]](#cite_note-263)

#### Chile

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Indigenous_peoples_of_the_Americas&action=edit&section=33 "Edit section: Chile")]

Main article: [Indigenous peoples in Chile](/wiki/Indigenous_peoples_in_Chile "Indigenous peoples in Chile")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1a/Mapuche.jpg/250px-Mapuche.jpg)](/wiki/File:Mapuche.jpg)

A Mapuche man in present-day [Chile](/wiki/Chile "Chile")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/32/Hombre_%26_mujer_Mapuche.jpg/250px-Hombre_%26_mujer_Mapuche.jpg)](/wiki/File:Hombre_%26_mujer_Mapuche.jpg)

A Mapuche man and woman; the Mapuche make up about 85% of Indigenous population that live in Chile.

According to the 2012 Census, 10% of the Chilean population, including the [Rapa Nui](/wiki/Rapa_Nui_people "Rapa Nui people") (a [Polynesian people](/wiki/Polynesians "Polynesians")) of [Easter Island](/wiki/Easter_Island "Easter Island"), was Indigenous, although most show varying degrees of mixed heritage.[[263]](#cite_note-264) Many are descendants of the [Mapuche](/wiki/Mapuche "Mapuche") and live in [Santiago](/wiki/Santiago,_Chile "Santiago, Chile"), [Araucanía](/wiki/Araucan%C3%ADa_Region "Araucanía Region"), and [Los Lagos Region](/wiki/Los_Lagos_Region "Los Lagos Region"). The Mapuche successfully fought off defeat in the first 300–350 years of Spanish rule during the [Arauco War](/wiki/Arauco_War "Arauco War"). Relations with the new Chilean Republic were good until the Chilean state decided to occupy their lands. During the [Occupation of Araucanía](/wiki/Occupation_of_Araucan%C3%ADa "Occupation of Araucanía"), the Mapuche surrendered to the country's army in the 1880s. Their land was opened to settlement by Chileans and Europeans. [Conflict over Mapuche land rights](/wiki/Mapuche_conflict "Mapuche conflict") continues to the present.

Other groups include the [Aymara](/wiki/Aymara_people "Aymara people"), the majority of whom live in Bolivia and Peru, with smaller numbers in the [Arica-Parinacota](/wiki/Arica-Parinacota_Region "Arica-Parinacota Region") and [Tarapacá](/wiki/Tarapac%C3%A1_Region "Tarapacá Region") regions, and the [Atacama people](/wiki/Atacama_people "Atacama people") (*Atacameños*), who reside mainly in [El Loa](/wiki/El_Loa "El Loa").

#### Colombia

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Indigenous_peoples_of_the_Americas&action=edit&section=34 "Edit section: Colombia")]

Main article: [Indigenous peoples in Colombia](/wiki/Indigenous_peoples_in_Colombia "Indigenous peoples in Colombia")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e7/Guambia%2C_Colombia.jpg/250px-Guambia%2C_Colombia.jpg)](/wiki/File:Guambia,_Colombia.jpg)

[Guambía people](/wiki/Guamb%C3%ADa_people "Guambía people") relaxing in [Colombia](/wiki/Colombia "Colombia")

A minority today within [Colombia](/wiki/Colombia "Colombia")'s mostly [mestizo](/wiki/Mestizo "Mestizo") and [White Colombian](/wiki/White_Colombian "White Colombian") population, Indigenous peoples living in Colombia, consist of around 85 distinct cultures and around 1,905,617 people, however, it is likely much higher.[[264]](#cite_note-265)[[265]](#cite_note-266) A variety of [collective rights](/wiki/Collective_rights "Collective rights") for Indigenous peoples are recognized in the 1991 Constitution. One of the influences is the [Muisca](/wiki/Muisca_people "Muisca people") culture, a subset of the larger [Chibcha](/wiki/Chibcha "Chibcha") ethnic group, famous for their use of gold, which led to the legend of *[El Dorado](/wiki/El_Dorado "El Dorado")*. At the time of the [Spanish conquest](/wiki/Spanish_conquest "Spanish conquest"), the Muisca were the largest Indigenous civilization geographically between the [Inca](/wiki/Inca_Empire "Inca Empire") and the [Aztec](/wiki/Aztec_Empire "Aztec Empire") empires.

#### Ecuador

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Indigenous_peoples_of_the_Americas&action=edit&section=35 "Edit section: Ecuador")]

Main article: [Indigenous peoples in Ecuador](/wiki/Indigenous_peoples_in_Ecuador "Indigenous peoples in Ecuador")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/08/Chaman_Amazonie_5_06.jpg/250px-Chaman_Amazonie_5_06.jpg)](/wiki/File:Chaman_Amazonie_5_06.jpg)

Shaman of the Cofán people from the [Amazonian forest](/wiki/Amazonian_forest "Amazonian forest") in present-day [Ecuador](/wiki/Ecuador "Ecuador")

[Ecuador](/wiki/Ecuador "Ecuador") was the site of many Indigenous cultures and civilizations of different proportions. An early sedentary culture, known as the [Valdivia culture](/wiki/Valdivia_culture "Valdivia culture"), developed in the coastal region, while the [Caras](/wiki/Caras_(tribe) "Caras (tribe)") and the [Quitus](/wiki/Quitu "Quitu") unified to form an elaborate civilization that ended at the birth of the Capital Quito. The [Cañaris](/wiki/Ca%C3%B1ari "Cañari") near [Cuenca](/wiki/Cuenca,_Ecuador "Cuenca, Ecuador") were the most advanced, and most feared by the [Inca](/wiki/Inca "Inca"), due to their fierce resistance to the Incan expansion. Their architectural remains were later destroyed by the Spaniards and the Incas.

Between 55% and 65% of Ecuador's population consists of Mestizos of mixed Indigenous and European ancestry, while Indigenous people comprise about 25%.[[266]](#cite_note-267) Genetic analysis indicates that Ecuadorian Mestizos are of predominantly Indigenous ancestry.[[267]](#cite_note-268) Approximately 96.4% of Ecuador's Indigenous population are Highland Quichuas living in the valleys of the Sierra region. Primarily consisting of the descendants of peoples conquered by the Incas, they are [Kichwa](/wiki/Kichwa "Kichwa") speakers and include the [Caranqui](/wiki/Inca-Caranqui "Inca-Caranqui"), the [Otavalos](/wiki/Otavalo_people "Otavalo people"), the Cayambe, the Quitu-Caras, the [Panzaleo](/wiki/Panzaleo "Panzaleo"), the Chimbuelo, the Salasacan, the Tugua, the Puruhá, the [Cañari](/wiki/Ca%C3%B1ari "Cañari"), and the [Saraguro](/wiki/Saraguro_people "Saraguro people"). Linguistic evidence suggests that the Salascan and the Saraguro may have been the descendants of Bolivian ethnic groups transplanted to Ecuador as *mitimaes*.

Coastal groups, including the [Awá](/wiki/Aw%C3%A1_(kwaiker) "Awá (kwaiker)"), [Chachi](/wiki/Ecuador "Ecuador"), and the [Tsáchila](/wiki/Ts%C3%A1chila "Tsáchila"), make up 0.24% percent of the Indigenous population, while the remaining 3.35 percent live in the Oriente and consist of the Oriente Kichwa (the Canelo and the Quijos), the [Shuar](/wiki/Shuar_people "Shuar people"), the [Huaorani](/wiki/Huaorani "Huaorani"), the Siona-Secoya, the [Cofán](/wiki/Cof%C3%A1n_people "Cofán people"), and the [Achuar](/wiki/Achuar_people "Achuar people").

In 1986, Indigenous peoples in Ecuador formed the first "truly" national [political organization](/wiki/Political_organisation "Political organisation"). [The Confederation of Indigenous Nationalities of Ecuador](/wiki/CONAIE "CONAIE") ([CONAIE](/wiki/CONAIE "CONAIE")) has been the primary political institution of Indigenous peoples since then and is now the second-largest political party in the nation. It has been influential in national politics, contributing to the ouster of presidents [Abdalá Bucaram](/wiki/Abdal%C3%A1_Bucaram "Abdalá Bucaram") in 1997 and [Jamil Mahuad](/wiki/Jamil_Mahuad "Jamil Mahuad") in 2000.

#### French Guiana

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Indigenous_peoples_of_the_Americas&action=edit&section=36 "Edit section: French Guiana")]

French Guiana is home to approximately 10,000 Indigenous peoples, such as the [Kalina](/wiki/Kalina_people "Kalina people") and [Lokono](/wiki/Lokono "Lokono"). Over time, the Indigenous population has protested against various environmental issues, such as illegal gold mining, pollution, and a drastic decrease in wild game.

#### Guyana

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Indigenous_peoples_of_the_Americas&action=edit&section=37 "Edit section: Guyana")]

Main article: [Indigenous peoples in Guyana](/wiki/Indigenous_peoples_in_Guyana "Indigenous peoples in Guyana")

During the early stages of colonization, the Indigenous peoples in Guyana partook in trade relations with Dutch settlers and assisted in militia services such as hunting down escaped slaves for the British, which continued until the 19th century. Indigenous Guyanese people are responsible for the invention of the [Guyanese pepperpot](/wiki/Guyanese_pepperpot "Guyanese pepperpot") and the foundation of the [Alleluia church](/wiki/Alleluia_church "Alleluia church").

Guyana's Indigenous peoples have been recognized under the Constitution of 1965 and comprise 9.16% of the overall population.

#### Paraguay

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Indigenous_peoples_of_the_Americas&action=edit&section=38 "Edit section: Paraguay")]

Main article: [Indigenous peoples in Paraguay](/wiki/Indigenous_peoples_in_Paraguay "Indigenous peoples in Paraguay")

The vast majority of Indigenous peoples in [Paraguay](/wiki/Paraguay "Paraguay") are concentrated in the [Gran Chaco](/wiki/Gran_Chaco "Gran Chaco") region in the northwest of the country, with the [Guaraní](/wiki/Guaran%C3%AD_people "Guaraní people") making up the majority of the Indigenous population in Paraguay. The [Guaraní language](/wiki/Guaran%C3%AD_language "Guaraní language") is recognized as an official language alongside Spanish, with approximately 90% of the population speaking Guaraní. The Indigenous population in Paraguay faces challenges such as low literacy rates and lack of safe drinking water or electricity.

#### Peru

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Indigenous_peoples_of_the_Americas&action=edit&section=39 "Edit section: Peru")]

Main article: [Indigenous peoples in Peru](/wiki/Indigenous_peoples_in_Peru "Indigenous peoples in Peru")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2c/Quechuawomanandchild.jpg/250px-Quechuawomanandchild.jpg)](/wiki/File:Quechuawomanandchild.jpg)

A [Quechua](/wiki/Quechuas "Quechuas") woman and child in the [Sacred Valley](/wiki/Sacred_Valley "Sacred Valley") in [Cuzco Region](/wiki/Cuzco_Region "Cuzco Region"), Peru

According to the [2017 Census](/wiki/2017_Peruvian_census "2017 Peruvian census"), the Indigenous population in Peru makes up approximately 26%.[[6]](#cite_note-census2017-7) However, this does not include mestizos of mixed Indigenous and European descent, who make up the majority of the population. Genetic testing indicates that Peruvian Mestizos are of predominantly Indigenous ancestry.[[268]](#cite_note-269) Indigenous traditions and customs have shaped the way Peruvians live and see themselves today. Cultural citizenship — or what Renato Rosaldo has called, "the right to be different and to belong, in a democratic, participatory sense" (1996:243) — is not yet very well developed in Peru. This is most apparent in the country's Amazonian region, where Indigenous societies continue to struggle against state-sponsored economic abuses, cultural discrimination, and pervasive violence.[[269]](#cite_note-270)

#### Suriname

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Indigenous_peoples_of_the_Americas&action=edit&section=40 "Edit section: Suriname")]

Main article: [Indigenous peoples in Suriname](/wiki/Indigenous_peoples_in_Suriname "Indigenous peoples in Suriname")

According to the 2012 census, the Indigenous population of Suriname numbers around 20,000, amounting to 3.8% of the population. The most numerous Indigenous groups in Suriname primarily comprise the [Lokono](/wiki/Lokono "Lokono"), [Kalina](/wiki/Kalina_people "Kalina people"), [Tiriyó](/wiki/Tiriy%C3%B3_people "Tiriyó people"), and [Wayana](/wiki/Wayana "Wayana").

#### Uruguay

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Indigenous_peoples_of_the_Americas&action=edit&section=41 "Edit section: Uruguay")]

Main article: [Indigenous peoples in Uruguay](/wiki/Indigenous_peoples_in_Uruguay "Indigenous peoples in Uruguay")

In the 2023 census, 6.4% of the population in Uruguay claimed to have some degree of indigenous ancestry.[[270]](#cite_note-271)

#### Venezuela

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Indigenous_peoples_of_the_Americas&action=edit&section=42 "Edit section: Venezuela")]

Main article: [Indigenous peoples in Venezuela](/wiki/Indigenous_peoples_in_Venezuela "Indigenous peoples in Venezuela")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/87/A_Warao_family_in_their_canoe.jpg/250px-A_Warao_family_in_their_canoe.jpg)](/wiki/File:A_Warao_family_in_their_canoe.jpg)

A [Warao family](/wiki/Warao_people "Warao people") traveling in their canoe in [Venezuela](/wiki/Venezuela "Venezuela")

Most Venezuelans have some degree of Indigenous heritage even if they may not identify as such. The 2011 census estimated that around 52% of the population identified as [mestizo](/wiki/Mestizo_Venezuelans "Mestizo Venezuelans"). But those who identify as Indigenous, from being raised in those cultures, make up only around 2% of the total population. The Indigenous peoples speak around 29 different languages and many more dialects. As some of the ethnic groups are very small, their native languages are in danger of becoming extinct in the next decades. The most important Indigenous groups are the [Ye'kuana](/wiki/Ye%27kuana "Ye'kuana"), the [Wayuu](/wiki/Wayuu "Wayuu"), the [Kali'na](/wiki/Kali%27na "Kali'na"), the [Ya̧nomamö](/wiki/Yanomami "Yanomami"), the [Pemon](/wiki/Pemon "Pemon"), and the [Warao](/wiki/Warao_people "Warao people"). The most advanced Indigenous peoples to have lived within the boundaries of present-day Venezuela are thought to have been the [Timoto-cuicas](/wiki/Timoto-cuicas "Timoto-cuicas"), who lived in the Venezuelan Andes. Historians estimate that there were between 350,000 and 500,000 Indigenous inhabitants at the time of Spanish colonization. The most densely populated area was the Andean region (Timoto-cuicas), thanks to their advanced agricultural techniques and ability to produce a surplus of food.

The 1999 [constitution of Venezuela](/wiki/Constitution_of_Venezuela "Constitution of Venezuela") gives Indigenous peoples [special rights](/wiki/Special_rights "Special rights"), although the vast majority of them still live in very critical conditions of poverty. The government provides primary education in their languages in public schools to some of the largest groups, in efforts to continue the languages.

### Caribbean

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Indigenous_peoples_of_the_Americas&action=edit&section=43 "Edit section: Caribbean")]

Main article: [Indigenous peoples of the Caribbean](/wiki/Indigenous_peoples_of_the_Caribbean "Indigenous peoples of the Caribbean")

The Indigenous population of the Caribbean islands consisted of the [Taíno](/wiki/Ta%C3%ADno "Taíno") of the [Lucayan Archipelago](/wiki/Lucayan_Archipelago "Lucayan Archipelago"), the [Greater Antilles](/wiki/Greater_Antilles "Greater Antilles") and the northern [Lesser Antilles](/wiki/Lesser_Antilles "Lesser Antilles"), the [Kalinago](/wiki/Kalinago "Kalinago") of the Lesser Antilles, the [Ciguayo](/wiki/Ciguayo "Ciguayo") and [Macorix](/wiki/Macorix_language "Macorix language") of parts of [Hispaniola](/wiki/Hispaniola "Hispaniola"), and the [Guanahatabey](/wiki/Guanahatabey "Guanahatabey") of western [Cuba](/wiki/Cuba "Cuba"). The overall population suffered the most adverse colonial effects out of all the Indigenous populations in the Americas, as the Kalinago have been reduced to a few islands in the Lesser Antilles such as [Dominica](/wiki/Dominica "Dominica") and the Taíno are culturally extinct, though a large proportion of populations in Greater Antillean islands such as [Puerto Rico](/wiki/Puerto_Rico "Puerto Rico") and Cuba to a lesser extent,[[271]](#cite_note-272) possesses degrees of Taíno ancestry. The [Cayman Islands](/wiki/Cayman_Islands "Cayman Islands") were the only island group in the Caribbean to have remained unsettled by Indigenous peoples before the colonial era.[[272]](#cite_note-273)

### Asia

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Indigenous_peoples_of_the_Americas&action=edit&section=44 "Edit section: Asia")]

Main article: [Latin American Asian](/wiki/Latin_American_Asian "Latin American Asian")

#### Philippines

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Indigenous_peoples_of_the_Americas&action=edit&section=45 "Edit section: Philippines")]

Main article: [Mexican settlement in the Philippines](/wiki/Mexican_settlement_in_the_Philippines "Mexican settlement in the Philippines")

Historically, during the Spanish colonization of the Philippines, the territory was ruled as a province of the Mexico-centered [Viceroyalty of New Spain](/wiki/Viceroyalty_of_New_Spain "Viceroyalty of New Spain") and thus many Mexicans including those of Indgenous [Aztec](/wiki/Aztec_people "Aztec people") and [Tlaxcalan](/wiki/Tlaxcala "Tlaxcala") descent, were sent as colonists there.[[273]](#cite_note-Mehl-2016-274): [Chpt. 6](https://web.archive.org/web/20180614082235/https://www.cambridge.org/core/books/forced-migration-in-the-spanish-pacific-world/unruly-mexicans-in-manila/EF2599210A0715A5A91B23BB9D84B96C) According to a genetic study by the [National Geographic](/wiki/National_Geographic "National Geographic"), Filipinos can trace an average of 2% of their ancestry to Native Americans.[[274]](#cite_note-NatGeo-275)[[275]](#cite_note-JournalHuman-276)
