Since the late 20th century, Indigenous peoples in the Americas have become more politically active in asserting their treaty rights and expanding their influence. Some have organized to achieve some sort of [self-determination](/wiki/Self-determination "Self-determination") and preservation of their cultures. Organizations such as the [Coordinator of Indigenous Organizations of the Amazon River Basin](/wiki/Coordinator_of_Indigenous_Organizations_of_the_Amazon_River_Basin "Coordinator of Indigenous Organizations of the Amazon River Basin") and the Indian Council of South America are examples of movements that are overcoming national borders to reunite Indigenous populations, for instance, those across the [Amazon Basin](/wiki/Amazon_Basin "Amazon Basin"). Similar movements for Indigenous rights can also be seen in Canada and the United States, with movements like the [International Indian Treaty Council](/wiki/International_Indian_Treaty_Council "International Indian Treaty Council") and the accession of native Indigenous groups into the [Unrepresented Nations and Peoples Organization](/wiki/Unrepresented_Nations_and_Peoples_Organization "Unrepresented Nations and Peoples Organization").

There has been a recognition of Indigenous movements on an international scale. The membership of the [United Nations](/wiki/United_Nations "United Nations") voted to adopt the [Declaration on the Rights of Indigenous Peoples](/wiki/Declaration_on_the_Rights_of_Indigenous_Peoples "Declaration on the Rights of Indigenous Peoples"), despite dissent from some of the stronger countries of the Americas.

In Colombia, various Indigenous groups have protested the denial of their rights. People organized a march in [Cali](/wiki/Cali "Cali") in October 2008 to demand the government live up to promises to protect Indigenous lands, defend the Indigenous against violence, and reconsider the [free trade](/wiki/Free_trade "Free trade") pact with the United States.[[276]](#cite_note-277)

### Indigenous heads of state

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Indigenous_peoples_of_the_Americas&action=edit&section=47 "Edit section: Indigenous heads of state")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/51/Presidentes_del_Per%C3%BA_y_Bolivia_inauguran_Encuentro_Presidencial_y_III_Gabinete_Binacional_Per%C3%BA-Bolivia_%2836962597345%29_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-Presidentes_del_Per%C3%BA_y_Bolivia_inauguran_Encuentro_Presidencial_y_III_Gabinete_Binacional_Per%C3%BA-Bolivia_%2836962597345%29_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Presidentes_del_Per%C3%BA_y_Bolivia_inauguran_Encuentro_Presidencial_y_III_Gabinete_Binacional_Per%C3%BA-Bolivia_(36962597345)_(cropped).jpg)

[Evo Morales](/wiki/Evo_Morales "Evo Morales"), an [Aymara](/wiki/Aymara_people "Aymara people") member and former [President of Bolivia](/wiki/President_of_Bolivia "President of Bolivia")

The first Indigenous President of the Americas was [José María Melo](/wiki/Jos%C3%A9_Mar%C3%ADa_Melo "José María Melo"), of [Pijao](/wiki/Pijao_people "Pijao people") descent, who led Colombia in 1854 starting on 17 April 1854. José was born on 9 October 1800, in [Chaparral, Tolima](/wiki/Chaparral,_Tolima "Chaparral, Tolima"), and before his presidency, he fought alongside [Simon Bolivar](/wiki/Simon_Bolivar "Simon Bolivar") in the [Spanish-American Wars of Independence](/wiki/Spanish_American_wars_of_independence "Spanish American wars of independence"). José María Melo led the [Republic of New Granada](/wiki/Republic_of_New_Granada "Republic of New Granada") during the [Colombian Civil War of 1854](https://es.wikipedia.org/wiki/Guerra_civil_colombiana_de_1854 "es:Guerra civil colombiana de 1854") but eventually lost and was exiled on 4 December 1854.[[277]](#cite_note-tiempo-278)

The first Indigenous candidate to be democratically elected as head of a country in the Americas was [Benito Juárez](/wiki/Benito_Ju%C3%A1rez "Benito Juárez"), a [Zapotec](/wiki/Zapotec_peoples "Zapotec peoples") Mexican who was elected President of Mexico in 1858 and led the country until 1872 and led the country to victory during the [Second French intervention in Mexico](/wiki/Second_French_intervention_in_Mexico "Second French intervention in Mexico").[[278]](#cite_note-279)

In 1930 [Luis Miguel Sánchez Cerro](/wiki/Luis_Miguel_S%C3%A1nchez_Cerro "Luis Miguel Sánchez Cerro") became the first Peruvian President with [Indigenous Peruvian](/wiki/Indigenous_Peruvians "Indigenous Peruvians") ancestry and the first in South America.[[279]](#cite_note-280) He came to power in a military coup.

In [2005](/wiki/2005_Bolivian_general_election "2005 Bolivian general election"), [Evo Morales](/wiki/Evo_Morales "Evo Morales") of the [Aymara people](/wiki/Aymara_people "Aymara people") was the first Indigenous candidate elected as [president of Bolivia](/wiki/President_of_Bolivia "President of Bolivia") and the first elected in South America.[[280]](#cite_note-281)

## Genetic research

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Indigenous_peoples_of_the_Americas&action=edit&section=48 "Edit section: Genetic research")]

Main article: [Genetic history of Indigenous peoples of the Americas](/wiki/Genetic_history_of_Indigenous_peoples_of_the_Americas "Genetic history of Indigenous peoples of the Americas")

See also: [Y-DNA haplogroups in Indigenous peoples of the Americas](/wiki/Y-DNA_haplogroups_in_Indigenous_peoples_of_the_Americas "Y-DNA haplogroups in Indigenous peoples of the Americas")

[![Schematic illustration of maternal geneflow in and out of Beringia. Colours of the arrows correspond to approximate timing of the events and are decoded in the coloured time-bar. The initial peopling of Berinigia (depicted in light yellow) was followed by a standstill after which the ancestors of Indigenous Americans spread swiftly throughout the New World while some of the Beringian maternal lineages, such as C1a, spread westwards. More recent genetic exchange (shown in green) is manifested by back-migration of A2a into Siberia and the spread of D2a into the Northeastern United States that post-dates the initial arrival of people in the New World.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/67/Map_of_gene_flow_in_and_out_of_Beringia.jpg/250px-Map_of_gene_flow_in_and_out_of_Beringia.jpg)](/wiki/File:Map_of_gene_flow_in_and_out_of_Beringia.jpg)

A schematic illustration of maternal (mtDNA) gene-flow in and out of Beringia, from 25,000 years ago to present

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/40/First_peopling_of_America.png/250px-First_peopling_of_America.png)](/wiki/File:First_peopling_of_America.png)

A map showing the origin of the first wave of humans into the [Americas](/wiki/Americas "Americas"), including the Ancestral Northern Eurasian, which represent a distinct Paleolithic Siberian population, and the Northeast Asians, which are an East Asian-related group. The admixture happened somewhere in Northeast [Siberia](/wiki/Siberia "Siberia").[[281]](#cite_note-282)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/cb/PCA_of_the_native_populations_of_Sakha_in_the_context_of_other_Eurasian_and_American_populations.jpg/250px-PCA_of_the_native_populations_of_Sakha_in_the_context_of_other_Eurasian_and_American_populations.jpg)](/wiki/File:PCA_of_the_native_populations_of_Sakha_in_the_context_of_other_Eurasian_and_American_populations.jpg)

Principal component analysis showing the Native American cluster in other Eurasian populations.[[282]](#cite_note-283)

[Genetic history of Indigenous peoples of the Americas](/wiki/Genetic_history_of_Indigenous_peoples_of_the_Americas "Genetic history of Indigenous peoples of the Americas") primarily focuses on [Human Y-chromosome DNA haplogroups](/wiki/Human_Y-chromosome_DNA_haplogroups "Human Y-chromosome DNA haplogroups") and [Human mitochondrial DNA haplogroups](/wiki/Human_mitochondrial_DNA_haplogroups "Human mitochondrial DNA haplogroups"). "Y-DNA" is passed solely along the [patrilineal](/wiki/Patrilineal "Patrilineal") line, from father to son, while "mtDNA" is passed down the [matrilineal](/wiki/Matrilineal "Matrilineal") line, from mother to offspring of both sexes. Neither [recombines](/wiki/Genetic_recombination "Genetic recombination") and thus Y-DNA and mtDNA change only by chance mutation at each generation with no intermixture between parents' genetic material.[[283]](#cite_note-nomenclature-284) [Autosomal](/wiki/Autosome "Autosome") "atDNA" markers are also used but differ from mtDNA or Y-DNA in that they overlap significantly.[[284]](#cite_note-Griffiths-285) AtDNA is generally used to measure the average continent-of-ancestry [genetic admixture](/wiki/Genetic_admixture "Genetic admixture") in the entire [human genome](/wiki/Human_genome "Human genome") and related [isolated populations](/wiki/Population_bottleneck "Population bottleneck").[[284]](#cite_note-Griffiths-285)

Genetic comparisons of the mitochondrial DNA (mtDNA) and Y-chromosome of Native Americans to that of certain [Siberian](/wiki/Siberian "Siberian") and [Central Asian](/wiki/Central_Asia "Central Asia") peoples (specifically [Paleo-Siberians](/wiki/Ancient_Paleo-Siberian "Ancient Paleo-Siberian"), [Turkic](/wiki/Turkic_people "Turkic people"), and historically the [Okunev culture](/wiki/Okunev_culture "Okunev culture")) have led Russian researcher I.A. Zakharov to believe that, among all the previously studied Asian peoples, it is "the peoples living between [Altai](/wiki/Altai_Mountains "Altai Mountains") and [Lake Baikal](/wiki/Lake_Baikal "Lake Baikal") along the [Sayan mountains](/wiki/Sayan_mountains "Sayan mountains") that are genetically closest to" Indigenous Americans.[[285]](#cite_note-286)

Some scientific evidence links them to North Asian peoples, specifically the [Indigenous peoples of Siberia](/wiki/Indigenous_peoples_of_Siberia "Indigenous peoples of Siberia"), such as the [Ket](/wiki/Ket_people "Ket people"), [Selkup](/wiki/Selkup_people "Selkup people"), [Chukchi](/wiki/Chukchi_people "Chukchi people"), and [Koryak](/wiki/Koryaks "Koryaks") peoples. Indigenous peoples of the Americas have been linked to some extent to North Asian populations by the distribution of [blood types](/wiki/Blood_types "Blood types"), and in [genetic composition](/wiki/Genetic_code "Genetic code") as reflected by [molecular](/wiki/Molecule "Molecule") data, and limited [DNA](/wiki/DNA "DNA") studies.[[286]](#cite_note-Jones2002-287)[[287]](#cite_note-SureshGLP-288)[[288]](#cite_note-CareyNHGRI-289)

The common occurrence of the [Asian](/wiki/Asian_people "Asian people") mtDNA haplogroups [A](/wiki/Haplogroup_A_(mtDNA) "Haplogroup A (mtDNA)"), [B](/wiki/Haplogroup_B_(mtDNA) "Haplogroup B (mtDNA)"), [C](/wiki/Haplogroup_C_(mtDNA) "Haplogroup C (mtDNA)"), and [D](/wiki/Haplogroup_D_(mtDNA) "Haplogroup D (mtDNA)") among eastern Asian and Native American populations has been noted.[[289]](#cite_note-Schurr2000-290) Some subclades of C and D that have been found in the limited populations of Native Americans who have agreed to DNA testing[[287]](#cite_note-SureshGLP-288)[[288]](#cite_note-CareyNHGRI-289) bear some resemblance to the C and D subclades in Mongolian, [Amur](/wiki/Amur_Oblast "Amur Oblast"), Japanese, Korean, and [Ainu](/wiki/Ainu_people "Ainu people") populations.[[289]](#cite_note-Schurr2000-290)[[290]](#cite_note-Starikovskayaetal2005-291)

Available genetic patterns lead to two main theories of genetic episodes affecting the Indigenous peoples of the Americas; first with the initial peopling of the Americas, and secondly with [European colonization of the Americas](/wiki/European_colonization_of_the_Americas "European colonization of the Americas").[[291]](#cite_note-SpencerWells-292)[[292]](#cite_note-Genebase-293)[[293]](#cite_note-294) The former is the determinant factor for the number of [gene](/wiki/Gene "Gene") lineages, [zygosity](/wiki/Zygosity "Zygosity") mutations, and founding [haplotypes](/wiki/Haplotype "Haplotype") present in today's Indigenous peoples of the Americas [populations](/wiki/Population_history_of_Indigenous_peoples_of_the_Americas "Population history of Indigenous peoples of the Americas").[[292]](#cite_note-Genebase-293)

The most popular theory among anthropologists is the [Bering Strait theory](/wiki/Bering_Strait_theory "Bering Strait theory"), of human settlement of the [New World](/wiki/New_World "New World") occurring in stages from the [Bering Sea coastline](/wiki/Bering_Sea "Bering Sea"), with a possible initial layover of 10,000 to 20,000 years in [Beringia](/wiki/Beringia "Beringia") for the small [founding population](/wiki/Founder_effect "Founder effect").[[294]](#cite_note-SpencerWells2-295)[[295]](#cite_note-First-296)[[296]](#cite_note-first2-297) The [micro-satellite](/wiki/Microsatellite_(genetics) "Microsatellite (genetics)") diversity and distributions of the Y lineage specific to South America indicate that certain Indigenous peoples of the Americas populations have been isolated since the initial colonization of the region.[[297]](#cite_note-subclades-298) The [Na-Dené](/wiki/Na-Den%C3%A9 "Na-Dené"), [Inuit](/wiki/Inuit "Inuit"), and [Indigenous populations of Alaska](/wiki/Alaska_Natives "Alaska Natives") exhibit [haplogroup Q (Y-DNA)](/wiki/Haplogroup_Q_(Y-DNA) "Haplogroup Q (Y-DNA)") mutations, however are distinct from other Indigenous peoples of the Americas with various mtDNA and atDNA mutations.[[298]](#cite_note-NaDene-299)[[299]](#cite_note-Zhivotovsky-300)[[300]](#cite_note-inuit-301) This suggests that the earliest migrants into the northern extremes of North America including Greenland derived from later migrant populations.[[301]](#cite_note-302)[[302]](#cite_note-Nadene1-303)

Multiple recent findings on autosomal DNA and full genome revealed more information about the formation, settlement, and external relationships of the Indigenous peoples of the Americas to other populations. Native Americans are very closely related to the [Paleosiberian tribes of Siberia](/wiki/Indigenous_peoples_of_Siberia "Indigenous peoples of Siberia"), and to the ancient samples of the [Mal'ta–Buret' culture](/wiki/Mal%27ta%E2%80%93Buret%27_culture "Mal'ta–Buret' culture") ([Ancient North Eurasians](/wiki/Ancient_North_Eurasians "Ancient North Eurasians")) as well as to the [Ancient Beringians](/wiki/Ancient_Beringians "Ancient Beringians"). Native Americans also share a relatively higher genetic affinity with [East Asian peoples](/wiki/East_Asian_peoples "East Asian peoples"). Native American genetic ancestry is occasionally dubbed as "Amerindian". This type of ancestry largely overlaps with "Paleosiberian" ancestry but is differentiated from "Neo-Siberian" ancestry, which represents historical expansions from Northeast Asia and is today widespread among Siberian populations. The ancestors of Native Americans used a single migration route, most likely through Beringia, and subsequently populated all of the Americas in a time range between 25,000 and 15,000 years ago. Possible contact between Native Americans and Polynesians dates back to 1,400 years ago. Previously hypothesized "Paleo-Indian" groups turned out to be genetically identical to modern Native Americans. The controversial claim that the first peoples came from Europe via the North Atlantic, based on an ostensible similarity in stone-tool technology between the Solutrean culture of Pleistocene Europe and Clovis in North America, was undermined by the genome of the Anzick Clovis child, which sits squarely on the branch of Ancestral Native American peoples. No ancient or present-day genome (or mtDNA or Y chromosome marker) in the Americas has shown any direct affinities to Upper Palaeolithic European populations.[[303]](#cite_note-304)[[304]](#cite_note-305)[[305]](#cite_note-306)[[306]](#cite_note-307)[[307]](#cite_note-308)[[308]](#cite_note-309)[[309]](#cite_note-310)[[310]](#cite_note-311)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/27/QpGraph_Maier_et_al._2023_ANE.jpg/250px-QpGraph_Maier_et_al._2023_ANE.jpg)](/wiki/File:QpGraph_Maier_et_al._2023_ANE.jpg)

A qpGraph on the formation of Ancient Paleo-Siberians and Native American populations.[[311]](#cite_note-312)

The date for the [gene pool](/wiki/Gene_pool "Gene pool") formation of Indigenous Americans ranges from 36,000 to 25,000 years ago, with their internal diverging being around 21,000 years ago, during the settlement of the Americas.[[312]](#cite_note-313) Native Americans formed from the admixture of a lineage that diverged from [Ancient East Asian people](/wiki/East_Asian_people "East Asian people") around 36,000 years ago somewhere in [Southern China](/wiki/Southern_China "Southern China"), and subsequently migrated northwards into Siberia where they merged with a Paleolithic Siberian population known as [Ancient North Eurasians](/wiki/Ancient_North_Eurasians "Ancient North Eurasians") (ANE), deeply related to European hunter-gatherers, giving rise to both [Indigenous peoples of Siberia](/wiki/Indigenous_peoples_of_Siberia "Indigenous peoples of Siberia") and Ancestral Native Americans. Both Paleo-Siberians and Ancestral Native Americans derive between 32 and 44% of their ancestry from Ancient North Eurasians (ANE), and 56–68% ancestry from Ancient East Asians.[[313]](#cite_note-314)[[314]](#cite_note-315)[[315]](#cite_note-316)[[316]](#cite_note-317) Based on a 2023 mitochondrial DNA study, a subsequent wave of migration from [Northern China](/wiki/North_China "North China"), originating near the present-day cities of Beijing and Tianjin, occurred as recently as 9000 BCE, following a previously unknown coastal route from Asia to America.[[317]](#cite_note-9000_BCE-318)

## Demography

Further information: [Population history of Indigenous peoples of the Americas](/wiki/Population_history_of_Indigenous_peoples_of_the_Americas "Population history of Indigenous peoples of the Americas")

The following table provides estimates for each country or territory in the Americas of the populations of Indigenous people and those with partial Indigenous ancestry, each expressed as a percentage of the overall population. The total percentage obtained by adding both of these categories is also given.

*Note:* these categories are inconsistently defined and measured differently from country to country. Some figures are based on the results of population-wide genetic surveys, while others are based on self-identification or observational estimation.

275. **[^](#cite_ref-JournalHuman_276-0)** Go MC, Jones AR, Algee-Hewitt B, Dudzik B, Hughes C (2019). ["Classification Trends among Contemporary Filipino Crania Using Fordisc 3.1"](https://www.academia.edu/38744342). *Human Biology*. **2** (4). University of Florida Press: 293–303. [doi](/wiki/Doi_(identifier) "Doi (identifier)"):[10.5744/fa.2019.1005](https://doi.org/10.5744%2Ffa.2019.1005). [S2CID](/wiki/S2CID_(identifier) "S2CID (identifier)") [159266278](https://api.semanticscholar.org/CorpusID:159266278). [Archived](https://web.archive.org/web/20210107133718/https://www.academia.edu/38744342/Classification_Trends_Among_Modern_Filipino_Crania_Using_Fordisc_3_1) from the original on 7 January 2021. Retrieved 13 September 2020. "[Page 1] ABSTRACT: Filipinos represent a significant contemporary demographic group globally, yet they are underrepresented in the forensic anthropological literature. Given the complex population history of the Philippines, it is important to ensure that traditional methods for assessing the biological profile are appropriate when applied to these peoples. Here we analyze the classification trends of a modern Filipino sample (n = 110) when using the Fordisc 3.1 (FD3) software. We hypothesize that Filipinos represent an admixed population drawn largely from Asian and marginally from European parental gene pools, such that FD3 will classify these individuals morphometrically into reference samples that reflect a range of European admixture, in quantities from small to large. Our results show the greatest classification into Asian reference groups (72.7%), followed by Hispanic (12.7%), Indigenous American (7.3%), African (4.5%), and European (2.7%) groups included in FD3. This general pattern did not change between males and females. Moreover, replacing the raw craniometric values with their shape variables did not significantly alter the trends already observed. These classification trends for Filipino crania provide useful information for casework interpretation in forensic laboratory practice. Our findings can help biological anthropologists to better understand the evolutionary, population historical, and statistical reasons for FD3-generated classifications. The results of our study indicate that ancestry estimation in forensic anthropology would benefit from population-focused research that gives consideration to histories of colonialism and periods of admixture."

"Our finding of no excess allele sharing with non-Native American populations in the ancient samples is also striking as many of these individuals—including those at Lapa do Santo—have a "Paleoamerican" cranial morphology that has been suggested to be evidence of the spread of a substructured population of at least two different Native American source populations from Asia to the Americas (von Cramon-Taubadel et al., 2017). Our finding that early Holocene individuals with such a morphology are consistent with deriving all their ancestry from the same homogeneous ancestral population as other Native Americans extends the finding of Raghavan et al., 2015, who came to a similar conclusion after analyzing Native Americans inferred to have Paleoamerican morphology who lived within the last millennium."

[Bibcode](/wiki/Bibcode_(identifier) "Bibcode (identifier)"):[2021Natur.594..356W](https://ui.adsabs.harvard.edu/abs/2021Natur.594..356W). "It is now evident that the initial dispersal involved the movement from northeast Asia. The first peoples, once south of the continental ice sheets, spread widely, expanded rapidly, and branched into multiple populations. Their descendants—over the next fifteen millennia—experienced varying degrees of isolation, admixture, continuity and replacement, and their genomes help to illuminate the relationships among major subgroups of Native American populations. Notably, all ancient individuals in the Americas, save for later-arriving Arctic peoples, are more closely related to contemporary Indigenous American individuals than to any other population elsewhere, which challenges the claim—which is based on anatomical evidence—that there was an early, non-Native American population in the Americas."
